input: FINDINGS: There is mild atelectasis within the bilateral lung bases. Centrilobular emphysematous changes are subtly seen within the bilateral lung bases. Redemonstration of scarring and architectural distortion within the right lower lobe, unchanged compared to the prior study. No focal airspace consolidation. No pleural or pericardial effusions. The heart size appears grossly within normal limits. There is redemonstration of a 2.1 cm hemangioma within the right lobe of the liver, segment 8, which is unchanged compared to the prior study. The remainder of the liver appears grossly unremarkable and unchanged. There is persistent intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation, which appears slightly increased in prominence compared to the prior study. The extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation also appears slightly increased compared to the prior study. The gallbladder appears decompressed and grossly unremarkable. The spleen, pancreas, bilateral adrenal glands, and bilateral kidneys also appear within normal limits. There is normal bilateral symmetrical renal enhancement and excretion of contrast. There is no hydronephrosis. There is stable appearance of a left adrenal nodule measuring 19 x 15 mm in diameter, which appears unchanged compared to prior studies, and was seen on a prior MRI study to represent an adrenal adenoma. No mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. The bowel appears unremarkable without wall thickening, inflammatory changes, or dilatation. No intraperitoneal free-air or free fluid. Persistent prominent left parametrial veins are again seen. The urinary bladder appears unremarkable. The visualized osseous structures appear grossly unchanged compared to the prior study. Stable appearance to a small sclerotic focus within the right aspect of the L3 vertebral body. IMPRESSION: 1. INTERVAL SLIGHT INCREASE IN THE APPEARANCE OF INTRAHEPATIC AND EXTRAHEPATIC BILIARY DUCTAL DILATATION, WITH THE COMMON DUCT MEASURING UP TO 9 MM IN DIAMETER. CORRELATION WITH LABORATORY VALUES IS RECOMMENDED. IF CLINICALLY INDICATED, AN ULTRASOUND OR MRI COULD BE OBTAINED FOR FURTHER EVALUATION. 2. STABLE APPEARANCE TO ARCHITECTURAL DISTORTION WITHIN THE RIGHT LOWER LOBE. MILD EMPHYSEMATOUS CHANGES ARE ALSO AGAIN PRESENT. 3. STABLE APPEARANCE TO A LEFT ADRENAL NODULE, CONSISTENT WITH AN ADRENAL ADENOMA AS GIVEN IN THE PATIENT'S PRIOR HISTORY. 4. STABLE APPEARANCE TO A HEPATIC HEMANGIOMA. 5. THE FINDINGS WERE DISCUSSED WITH Francis Delacruz BY THE RADIOLOGY RESIDENT ON-CALL AT 1730 HOURS ON THE EVENING OF 1-8-2009. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Mild atelectasis is present in the lung bases; there is no evidence of focal consolidation. No pleural or pericardial effusions. The heart size is within normal limits. There is a nasogastric tube with the tip in the gastric antrum. A few tiny low-attenuation lesions are seen in the liver, which are too small to definitively characterize but appear unchanged from the prior study from 12/29/2003 and likely represent cysts. No new, focal hepatic lesions are identified. The gallbladder, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys appear unremarkable. There are multiple dilated small bowel loops, measuring up to 4.8 cm in diameter. A transition point is identified in the left lower quadrant, just deep to the anterior abdominal wall musculature (series 4 image 260). The small bowel loops distal to this point appear decompressed. A small amount of free fluid is seen in the pelvis, which measures slightly higher than simple fluid attenuation. The bowel wall enhancement appears within normal limits. There is no evidence of bowel wall thickening, pneumatosis, or portal venous gas. Stool is seen in the colon without a significant amount of colonic fluid. The appendix is not definitely visualized, and the right lower quadrant is obscured by streak artifact from the metallic hardware in the right pelvis and hip. The pelvic organs and urinary bladder appear unremarkable. No evidence of pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in abdomen or pelvis. Postsurgical changes are seen in the right pelvis with multiple metallic screws. A right total hip arthroplasty is noted. An old fracture deformity is seen in the right inferior pubic ramus. IMPRESSION: 1. SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION WITH THE TRANSITION POINT IN THE LEFT LOWER QUADRANT, LIKELY SECONDARY TO POSTOPERATIVE ADHESIONS. THE SMALL BOWEL IS DILATED UP TO 4.8 CM AND THERE IS NO FLUID WITHIN THE CECUM; HOWEVER, THERE IS NO EVIDENCE OF ISCHEMIC CHANGES. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The heart is within normal size limits. No pericardial effusion. The distal descending thoracic aorta is normal in course and caliber. The distal esophagus and gastroesophageal junction are grossly unremarkable. No pleural effusions. Evaluation of the lung parenchyma demonstrates minimal dependent, reticular and ground glass opacities compatible with atelectasis. The liver is normal in size, contour and attenuation. No focal hepatic mass. The hepatic and portal veins are patent. The gallbladder is distended. The gallbladder wall is thickening with surrounding inflammatory stranding. There is irregular enhancement of the gallbladder wall. Mild intrahepatic hepatic biliary ductal dilatation with the common bile duct dilated to 9 mm. No stones or definite obstructing lesion identified. The adrenal glands are within normal limits. The spleen is normal in size, contour and attenuation. Moderate pancreatic duct dilatation up to 6 mm. Possible pancreas divisum. The kidneys are symmetric in size, contour, contrast enhancement and excretion. No focal renal mass, hydronephrosis or nephrolithiasis. Punctate hypo-densities within the renal parenchyma are too small to characterize, however statistically, likely represent renal cysts. The bladder appears unremarkable. Evaluation of the pelvis severely limited due to streak artifact from the bilateral hip arthroplasties. A pessary is seen in the pelvis. The stomach is grossly unremarkable. The small bowel is within normal limits without evidence of bowel wall thickening, hyperemia, pneumatosis or obstruction. The colon is normal in course and caliber. Scattered diverticula are present in the sigmoid colon without evidence of diverticulitis. The appendix is within normal limits. No free fluid within the abdomen or pelvis. The peritoneum is unremarkable. The abdominal aorta is normal in course and caliber. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes within the abdomen or pelvis. input: FINDINGS: Partial visualization of the lung bases demonstrate large calcified granuloma at the left base. There is scarring and atelectasis at the right lung base. Pleural calcifications seen bilaterally. Moderate centrilobular emphysema is present. Lung bases are otherwise clear. The liver, spleen, pancreas and adrenal glands are unremarkable. Old infarct of the inferior right and left kidney which has evolved compared to the prior study with prominent cortical thinning. No new areas of ischemic change are present. Ureters are unremarkable. Within the urinary bladder on post-contrast images, there is a 1 cm rounded area of increased density which was not clearly seen on the prior study. In addition, there is a small left-sided urinary bladder diverticulum. No evidence of obstruction. Small and large bowel are not dilated. No free air or free fluid within the abdomen. Prostate gland is enlarged with areas of calcification; no significant change. Arterial phase images demonstrate an infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm measuring 4.9 x 5 cm which is unchanged. The celiac axis, SMA and renal arteries are patent. There are two right and two left renal arteries with calcification at their origin; no significant stenosis. Extensive plaque at the iliac arteries bilaterally with no complete occlusion. There is a stent graft in place which is unchanged in position compared to the prior study. No evidence of hardware failure. On delayed images, there is no evidence of endoleak. IMPRESSION: 1. STABLE INFRARENAL ABDOMINAL AORTIC ANEURYSM. 2. UNCHANGED AORTOBI-ILIAC STENT GRAFT WITH NO EVIDENCE OF ENDOLEAK. 3. ROUNDED 1 CM DENSITY IN THE POSTERIOR URINARY BLADDER WHICH MAY REPRESENT ARTIFACT FROM EARLY CONTRAST FILLING; HOWEVER, A MASS LESION CANNOT BE EXCLUDED AND AN ULTRASOUND IS RECOMMENDED FOR FURTHER EVALUATION. 4. CALCIFIED PLEURAL PLAQUE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The cardiac silhouette is within normal size limits. Trace pericardial effusion.. The distal descending thoracic aorta normal in course and caliber and demonstrates atherosclerosis. The distal esophagus and gastroesophageal junction are grossly unremarkable. Interval resolution of the previously described pleural effusions. Evaluation of the lung parenchyma demonstrates minimal dependent, reticular and ground glass opacities compatible atelectasis. There is a nonspecific 2-mm pulmonary nodule the right middle lobe which was not definitely seen on the prior exam, however may have been secured by atelectasis series 7, image 43. The liver is abnormal in contour. There is relative atrophy of the right lobe of the liver with hypertrophy of the left lobe. Embolization coils are again noted in in the upper abdomen. Chemoembolization material is present in the right hepatic lobe. The perihepatic loculated fluid collection has decreased slightly in size. In comparison to the prior exam, the previously described heterogeneous, hypervascular, well circumscribed liver metastases have decreased slightly in size. Smaller representative lesions include a mass replacing the majority of the right hepatic lobe measuring approximately 10.0 cm in maximal transaxial diameter, previously measuring 10.8 cm series 5, image 72, a left hepatic mass which measures 4.1 x 2.6 cm, previously measuring 4.2 x 3.2 cm series 5, image 87, and left hepatic lesion measuring 1.6 x 1.2 cm, previously measuring 1.6 x 1.6 cm series 5, image 148. No intrahepatic biliary dilatation. The hepatic and portal veins are patent. The gallbladder is surgically absent. In comparison to the prior exam, the gas and fluid collection within the gallbladder fossa has resolved. The common bile duct is mildly dilated measuring 10 mm, previously measuring 7 mm in size, which may be related to patient's post cholecystectomy state and age. No radiopaque retained gallstones are identified. The adrenal glands are are within normal limits. The spleen is normal in size, contour and attenuation. The pancreas is within normal limits without evidence of mass or ductal dilatation. The kidneys are symmetric in size, contour, contrast enhancement and excretion. No focal renal mass, hydronephrosis or nephrolithiasis. Stable prominent right extrarenal pelvis. Punctate hypo-densities within the renal parenchyma are too small to characterize, however statistically, likely represent renal cysts. The bladder is unremarkable. Evaluation of the uterus demonstrates an exophytic, well circumscribed, heterogeneous mass measuring approximately 3.5 cm in size projecting from the right posterior lateral aspect of the uterus compatible with a fibroid. Calcifications are present in the uterine fundus suggestive of smaller uterine fibroids. No adnexal masses. A trace amount of free fluid is present in the pelvis. The stomach is grossly unremarkable. The small bowel is within normal limits without evidence of bowel wall thickening, hyperemia, pneumatosis or obstruction. The colon is normal in course and caliber. The abdominal aorta is normal in course and caliber and demonstrates atherosclerotic disease. Evaluation of the left lower quadrant again demonstrates a well-circumscribed, lobulated, heterogeneous, partially calcified mass which has also decreased slightly in size measuring 7.8 x 5.6 cm, previously measuring 7.9 x 5.8 cm series 5, image 162. The partially calcified mass in the anterior abdominal subcutaneous soft tissues has also decreased measuring 3.9 x 1.2 cm, previously measuring 4.1 x 1.4 cm series 5, image 161. Evaluation of the visualized osseous structures demonstrate multilevel degenerative change without focal suspicious lesion identified. IMPRESSION: 1. SLIGHT INTERVAL DECREASE IN SIZE OF LIVER, MESENTERIC AND ABDOMINAL WALL METASTATIC LESIONS. NO NEW METASTASES IDENTIFIED. 2. RESOLUTION OF ABSCESS WITHIN THE GALLBLADDER FOSSA. 3. RESOLUTION OF PLEURAL EFFUSIONS WITH SLIGHT INTERVAL DECREASE IN SIZE OF PERIHEPATIC FLUID COLLECTION. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Visualized heart and lung bases are unremarkable. The liver contains at least eight new parenchymal subcentimeter hypodensities worrisome for metastases, the largest measures 8mm in the right lobe (image 4/51). The ill-defined 3-cm mass in the pancreatic body is again noted, with distal duct dilatation up to approximately 4 mm. The splenic vein remains markedly narrowed at the site of this mass (series 4/96 and 4/94), and there are prominent collateral vessels in the upper abdomen. No evidence of superior mesenteric, splenic, portal or hepatic vein thrombosis. Since the prior exam, peritoneal carcinomatosis and ascites have increased. An enlarging perihepatic cystic implant measures 4-cm (series 4/61). Many of the soft tissue nodules in the peritoneal cavity demonstrate well-defined margins, and some hypodense components. A previously described left internal iliac 7-mm lymph node is unchanged (series 4/249). There are multiple air-fluid levels in the colon, with a persistent implant adjacent to the rectosigmoid junction (series 4/25 and 601/93, prior series 5/265), however there is no bowel obstruction. The spleen parenchyma, adrenal glands and left kidney are unremarkable. Stable subcentimeter hypodensities in the right kidney, which remain too small to characterize. No hydronephrosis. The urinary bladder is moderately distended, without focal abnormality. A enlarging sclerotic focus in the T10 vertebral body and right pedicle is compatible with metastasis (series 4/11-2-2005). IMPRESSION: 1. WORSENING CARCINOMATOSIS AND ASCITES, WITH ENLARGING HEPATIC PARENCHYMAL METASTASES, COMPATIBLE WITH PROGRESSION OF METASTATIC DISEASE. THERE IS NO BOWEL OBSTRUCTION. 2. PRIMARY PANCREATIC 3-CM MASS IS NOT SIGNIFICANTLY CHANGED. 3. T10 SCLEROTIC LESION CONSISTENT WITH METASTASIS. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The visualized lung bases show mild right lower lobe bronchial wall thickening and mucous plugging. Also seen is streaky bibasilar atelectasis. No pleural or pericardial effusion seen. An epiphrenic lymph node measures 6 mm (4/43). The liver is diffusely low in attenuation, compatible with steatosis. Overall liver morphology with nodular contour is consistent with cirrhosis. The liver parenchyma is heterogeneous with scattered vague foci of low attenuation. The largest is a roughly 12 mm hypodensity in segment 6 (3-11-2011/80) which is more conspicuous than on CT 2011, March 11th. The hepatic and portal veins are patent. Hepatic arterial anatomy is conventional. Again seen is evidence of portal venous hypertension, manifested as splenomegaly, small ascites, and perigastric varices. Multiple prominent periportal, portacaval, and gastrohepatic ligament lymph nodes are nonspecific. A vague wedge-shaped area of peripheral low attenuation in the inferior spleen may be a perfusion abnormality versus infarct. There is no biliary ductal dilation. The gallbladder is contracted. Gallbladder wall thickening is nonspecific and may be related to underlying liver disease. The pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys appear normal. The urinary bladder and pelvic organs are unremarkable. There are postsurgical findings in keeping with history of interval colectomy with right lower quadrant ileostomy and Hartmann's pouch. There is diffuse wall thickening, hyperemia, and submucosal edema of all small bowel loops. There is thickening and infiltration of the mesenteric leaves with trace interloop fluid. A proximal jejunal bowel loops in the left abdomen are mildly dilated to 3.2 cm. Fluid within the rectovesical pouch, measuring roughly 7.1 x 3.8 cm (4/244), may reflect dependent fluid with associated peritoneal enhancement versus loculated fluid collection with rim-enhancement. Prominent mesenteric lymph nodes are nonspecific, probably reactive. The principal vascular structures of the abdomen and pelvis are patent. The IMA is not visualized, as expected post colectomy. No suspicious osseous lesion identified. Midline anterior abdominal wall surgical incision and staples noted. IMPRESSION: 1. POSTSURGICAL FINDINGS IN KEEPING WITH HISTORY OF INTERVAL COLECTOMY WITH RIGHT LOWER QUADRANT ILEOSTOMY AND HARTMANN'S POUCH. 2. DIFFUSE HYPEREMIA AND AND WALL THICKENING OF BOWEL LOOPS ASSOCIATED WITH THICKENING AND INFILTRATION OF THE MESENTERIC LEAVES WITH INTERLOOP AND FREE FLUID IS NONSPECIFIC. FINDINGS MAY BE INFLAMMATORY, INFECTIOUS, OR LESS LIKELY REFLECT LOW FLOW ISCHEMIA. THE MESENTERIC VESSELS ARE PATENT. 3. FLUID WITHIN THE RECTOVESICAL POUCH MAY REFLECT DEPENDENT FLUID WITH ASSOCIATED PERITONEAL ENHANCEMENT VERSUS LOCULATED FLUID COLLECTION WITH RIM-ENHANCEMENT. 4. CIRRHOTIC AND FATTY LIVER WITH EVIDENCE OF PORTAL HYPERTENSION. SCATTERED VAGUE LOW ATTENUATION HEPATIC LESIONS ARE IDENTIFIED. THE LARGEST IS A ROUGHLY 12 MM HYPODENSITY IN SEGMENT 6, WHICH IS MORE CONSPICUOUS THAN ON CT 03-11. FURTHER EVALUATION WITH DEDICATED MULTIPHASE CT OR MR IS RECOMMENDED. 5. A VAGUE WEDGE-SHAPED AREA OF PERIPHERAL LOW ATTENUATION IN THE INFERIOR SPLEEN MAY BE A PERFUSION ABNORMALITY VERSUS INFARCT. ATTENTION ON FOLLOW-UP STUDIES. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is stable coarse peripheral reticulation with traction bronchiectasis present at the lung bases, compatible with pulmonary fibrosis. There is no pleural effusion. The heart appears normal in size. No pericardial effusion is demonstrated. Coronary atherosclerotic calcification is present. The liver appears normal in size and contour. Hepatic attenuation is diffusely diminished, compatible with hepatic steatosis. No intrahepatic mass is demonstrated. The hepatic and portal veins are patent. No intra-or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilation is present. The gallbladder appears normal. The pancreas demonstrates normal lobular architecture. There is punctate calcification within the distal pancreatic body, compatible with chronic pancreatitis. There is a 7-mm hypodense lesion within the adjacent parenchyma, possibly representing a pseudocyst or IPMN. No pancreatic ductal dilation is present. The spleen is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. The splenic vein is patent. The adrenal glands appear normal. The kidneys are normal in size. Irregularity of the bilateral renal contours may reflect prior infection or infarction. Right renal cysts are unchanged. There is no calculus or hydronephrosis. There is no obstruction to the excretion of contrast on delayed images. The urinary bladder is decompressed by a Foley catheter. The prostate gland appears normal in size and contains parenchymal calcifications. The abdominal aorta is tortuous and normal in caliber, with atherosclerotic calcification. The celiac, superior mesenteric, renal, and inferior mesenteric arteries are patent. There are no enlarged lymph nodes in the abdomen, pelvis, or inguinal regions by CT size criteria. Nonspecific subcentimeter paraesophageal nodes are present. A sliding hiatal hernia appears similar to the prior study. There is asymmetric submucosal edema within the wall of the gastroesophageal junction at the esophageal hiatus (series 4 image 25). There is a large 7.5-cm diameter gastric diverticulum containing air and fluid. The stomach is normal in caliber and wall thickness, although evaluation of the gastric antrum is limited by the limited distention. The small bowel and colon are normal in caliber. Colonic diverticulosis is present without CT evidence for diverticulitis. A large stool ball is present within the rectum, with mild rectal wall thickening (series 4 image 319). No free air or free fluid is present in the abdomen or pelvis. Multilevel degenerative changes of the spine are present. Vertebra plana at T12 is new since the prior study, but appears subacute to chronic as there is no perivertebral hemorrhage. Bilateral hip and sacroiliac joint degenerative changes are present. Left gynecomastia is evident. There is a small fat containing right inguinal hernia. IMPRESSION: 1. FECAL IMPACTION WITH. MILD RECTAL WALL THICKENING. NO PERIRECTAL INFLAMMATION OR BOWEL OBSTRUCTION. 2. SUBACUTE TO CHRONIC T12 VERTEBRA PLANA, NEW SINCE THE PRIOR CT. 3. PERIPHERAL PULMONARY RETICULATION WITH TRACTION BRONCHIECTASIS LIKELY REFLECTS PULMONARY FIBROSIS, INCOMPLETELY EVALUATED ON THIS STUDY. 4. HEPATIC STEATOSIS. 5. STABLE, LARGE GASTRIC DIVERTICULUM. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The visualized lung bases demonstrate dependent atelectasis / scarring. There are no pleural effusions. The heart is normal in size. Coronary arterial calcifications are present. The liver is homogeneous without focal enhancing masses. The hepatic vessels are patent. There is no intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The gallbladder is present. There is a 1.4 x 1.4 cm cystic lesion in the pancreatic body, and a 9-mm cystic lesion at the pancreatic tail, again most likely representing small side branch intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms, stable from prior study of SEPTEMBER 7, 19. The spleen and adrenal glands are unremarkable. The kidneys are normal in size and enhance symmetrically without hydronephrosis. There is a focal defect at the right posteromedial kidney (4/11), likely reflecting the sequela of prior infection / infarction. Subcentimeter hypodense lesions are seen in both kidneys which are too small to further characterize. Heavy sigmoid and descending colonic diverticulosis is noted. Additionally, the sigmoid colon is markedly thickened and edematous with significant pericolonic stranding, compatible with acute diverticulitis. There are additional foci of gas which may be within diverticula versus contained perforation. There is no abscess formation at this time. There is no large free intraperitoneal air. There is no evidence of obstruction. A normal appendix is identified in the right lower quadrant containing intraluminal gas. Several prominent lymph nodes are identified in the right pelvic and inguinal regions, some of which have increased in size from prior study. For example, a right external iliac lymph node has increased in size now measuring 12 mm (previously 6 mm). There has also been interval increase in size of two right inguinal lymph nodes, now measuring 14 and 21 mm (previously 12 and 13 mm respectively). This is likely reactive due to the patient's history of chronic right lower extremity cellulitis. There is no ascites. The prostate is normal in size. A TURP defect is identified. The urinary bladder is markedly distended and grossly unremarkable. Heavy atherosclerotic calcifications of the aorta and its branch vessels are identified. Postsurgical changes are again seen in both groins, with more increased infiltration of the right groin compatible with history of recent right iliofemoral endarterectomy. The visualized osseous structures demonstrate marked degenerative changes and a mild levoscoliosis of the lumbar spine. IMPRESSION: 1. HEAVY SIGMOID DIVERTICULOSIS WITH SIGNIFICANT INFLAMMATORY CHANGES COMPATIBLE WITH SIGMOID DIVERTICULITIS. NO DRAINABLE FLUID COLLECTION AT THIS TIME. NO LARGE FREE INTRAPERITONEAL AIR. 2. TWO STABLE PANCREATIC CYSTIC LESIONS, AGAIN LIKELY REPRESENTING SMALL SIDE BRANCH INTRADUCTAL PAPILLARY MUCINOUS NEOPLASMS (IPMN). 3. INTERVAL INCREASE IN SIZE OF RIGHT EXTERNAL ILIAC AND INGUINAL LYMPH NODES WHEN COMPARED TO PRIOR CT DATED 2019/10/02, LIKELY REACTIVE FROM THE PATIENT'S HISTORY OF CHRONIC RIGHT LOWER EXTREMITY CELLULITIS. 4. HEAVY ATHEROSCLEROTIC DISEASE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

output.anchor: small side branch intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms (observation:uncertain)

input: FINDINGS: Lungs: The lung bases are clear. Visualized portions of the heart are unremarkable. No pericardial effusion is seen. Vasculature: Status post abdominal aortic aneurysm repair with the stent graft extending from just inferior to the celiac takeoff through the bilateral external iliac arteries, with bilateral renal artery snorkeling. On the angiographic phase there is contrast seen outside of the stent in the juxtarenal position of the bilobed aneurysm. The large infrarenal aneurysm sac appears excluded and measures 7.3 x 6.6 cm, stable. Redemonstration of stenting in the left internal iliac artery, and redemonstration of an Amplatzer plug in the right internal iliac artery. The celiac and SMA remain patent. Embolization coils in the accessory left renal artery. Abdomen / pelvis: Multiple small hypoattenuating foci in the liver, which are too small to characterize. The gallbladder is unremarkable in appearance. No intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The pancreas, spleen, and adrenal glands are unremarkable the parents. Redemonstration of bilateral renal cysts. The small and large bowel are unremarkable. The bladder is collapsed limiting evaluation. The prostate is unremarkable in appearance. No abdominopelvic adenopathy is identified. Musculoskeletal: No suspicious osseous lesions identified. Addendum Begins *** Preliminary measurements were made during primary interpretation. PERSISTENT PRESUMABLY TYPE IA ENDOLEAK IN THE JUXTARENAL PORTION OF THE BILOBED ANEURYSM. THE LARGE INFRARENAL ANEURYSM SAC APPEARS EXCLUDED. input: FINDINGS: Respiratory motion partially limits exam quality in the lungs. Evaluation of the pelvis is limited due to metallic streak artifact from bilateral hip arthroplasty. Chest: There is no filling defect within the pulmonary artery or its main segmental and subsegmental branches within the limits of this exam to suggest pulmonary embolism. The heart and great vessels are unremarkable. No pericardial effusion. There is a right moderate pleural effusion. The central airways are patent. A right middle lobe 5-mm nodule is stable since 27th January. No new pulmonary nodules. No adenopathy in the chest. Abdomen and Pelvis: The liver is diffusely nodular with multiple well circumscribed hypodensities that are again noted. However, there is at least one new 3-mm hypodensity in segment 2 (series 7/29/2019) that is indeterminate. Of note, this exam is not tailored for hepatocellular carcinoma evaluation. Since the prior exam, ascites has increased and even extends into a right inguinal hernia, and there are areas of fascial thickening, for example along the left lower quadrant, however there are no areas of peritoneal enhancement. There are subcentimeter mesenteric lymph nodes. The spleen, gall bladder, adrenal glands and right kidney are unremarkable. Fatty atrophy of the pancreas. The left kidney contains a 4-mm nonobstructing stone. There is sigmoid diverticulosis, without diverticulitis. No bowel obstruction or abnormal bowel wall thickening. The urinary bladder is distended and partially obscured. No aggressive bony abnormality. Left lateral rib fractures demonstrate interval callus formation (series June 27th, 2031). Status post bilateral hip replacements. There is degenerative disk disease most pronounced at L5-S1. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM WITHIN THE LIMITS OF THIS EXAM. 2. MODERATE RIGHT PLEURAL EFFUSION AND SLIGHTLY INCREASED ASCITES. 3. CIRRHOSIS AND PORTAL HYPERTENSION. NEW LEFT HEPATIC 4-MM HYPODENSITY IT IS INDETERMINATE. PLEASE NOTE THIS EXAM IS NOT TAILORED FOR EVALUATION OF HEPATOCELLULAR CARCINOMA. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: VASCULATURE: Extensive atherosclerotic calcification of the aorta and its branches. There is calcification at the origins of both renal arteries, which are patent without significant stenosis. The celiac artery demonstrates marked calcification at the ostium, but is widely patent. Extensive calcifications of the splenic artery. An accessory left hepatic artery off the left gastric artery is noted. The superior mesenteric artery is calcified at its ostium and in its proximal segment, without significant stenosis or occlusion. There is a replaced right hepatic artery originating from the SMA. The inferior mesenteric artery is calcified and is nearly occluded. Severe atherosclerotic disease of the abdominal aorta extends into the iliacs and femoral arteries, without significant stenosis. CHEST: Evaluation of the lung bases demonstrates small bilateral pleural effusions, left greater than right, which are increased compared to prior study, with associated bilateral compressive atelectasis. There is a small hiatal hernia, unchanged. A nasogastric tube is in place with the tip in the distal stomach. Limited evaluation of the heart demonstrates cardiomegaly with at least biatrial enlargement. No pericardial effusions. Coronary calcifications are noted. A pacemaker is in place, but only partially included on this study. ABDOMEN: There is a small amount of ascites, which is new since the prior study. There is diffuse wall thickening of the large bowel, with some relative sparing of the proximal / mid transverse colon and the proximal sigmoid colon. Some fluid and stranding are noted in the right pericolic gutter. The normal appendix is visualized. There is a subcentimeter hypodense lesion in the right hepatic lobe, which is unchanged, and too small to characterize on this study. The gallbladder is markedly enlarged, increased in size compared to prior study, without evidence of wall thickening. There is a small amount of pericholecystic fluid, which may represent more evidence of ascites, seen in multiple locations. Multiple cystic lesions, arising from the pancreas are again seen, the largest of which arises from the neck and measures 6.4 cm in greatest dimension. All of these cysts appear stable in size. Two right renal cysts are again seen, the largest of which is in the lower pole, and both of which are stable in size compared to the prior study. The left renal vein is retroaortic and single. There is a Foley catheter in place in the bladder, which is decompressed, with a small amount of air present. MUSCULOSKELETAL: Evaluation of the osseous structures demonstrates extensive degenerative changes of the visualized thoracic and lumbar spine. IMPRESSION: 1. DIFFUSE WALL THICKENING OF THE LARGE BOWEL WITH SOME AREAS OF RELATIVE SPARING. INTERVAL DEVELOPMENT OF A MILD DEGREE OF ASCITES. FINDINGS ARE MOST CONCERNING FOR INFECTIOUS COLITIS. 2. EXTENSIVE ATHEROSCLEROTIC DISEASE OF THE AORTA AND ITS MAJOR BRANCHES, WITHOUT SIGNIFICANT STENOSIS OF THE CELIAC AND SUPERIOR MESENTERIC ARTERIES. THERE IS NEAR OCCLUSION OF THE ORIGIN OF THE INFERIOR MESENTERIC ARTERY. 3. STABLE APPEARANCE OF NUMEROUS CYSTIC PANCREATIC LESIONS. 4. SMALL BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS AND COMPRESSIVE ATELECTASIS, INCREASED COMPARED TO THE PRIOR STUDY. 5. MARKEDLY ENLARGED GALLBLADDER. IF THERE IS CLINICAL CONCERN FOR CHOLECYSTITIS, CONSIDER RIGHT UPPER QUADRANT ULTRASOUND. 6. THESE FINDINGS WERE DISCUSSED WITH THE CLINICAL TEAM BY THE ON CALL RESIDENT, md sweeney AT THE TIME OF THE EXAM. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Evaluation of the visceral organs, vasculature, and soft tissues is limited by lack of intravenous contrast. Diffuse fatty infiltration of liver, which is normal in size and smooth in contour. No gross biliary ductal dilatation, status post cholecystectomy. Moderate splenomegaly; spleen measures up to 15.3 cm in length on today's exam. Pancreas not well-evaluated. Normal adrenals. Bilateral perinephric stranding; no hydronephrosis nor nephroureterolithiasis (previous left hydronephrosis has resolved, status post interval removal of left ureteral stent). Bladder mildly distended, without obvious wall thickening. Prostate not enlarged. Bowel is of normal caliber. Appendix not definitively visualized. No ascites, loculated fluid collections, or lymphadenopathy. No aortobiiliac aneurysm; minimal vascular calcifications. Inferior vena caval filter again seen. No change, compared with the CT abdomen / pelvis dated MARCH 02, in 5-mm noncalcified pulmonary nodule within perifissural right lower lobe (image 13, series 3), or in 3-mm noncalcified pulmonary nodule within perifissural right middle lobe (image 18, series 3); given lack of interval change in these pulmonary nodules over a greater than five-year period, they are benign. No new findings within the lung bases, which are otherwise clear. No effusions; normal heart size. Status post median sternotomy. No suspicious bony lesions. Mild sclerosis of left sacroiliac joint (involving predominantly the left iliac side). Discogenic degenerative changes at lower lumbar spine / lumbosacral junction junction. IMPRESSION: 1. Diffuse fatty liver, within which steatohepatitis is not excluded; suggest clinical correlation with liver function tests. 2. Moderate splenomegaly, of uncertain etiology; suggest clinical correlation. 3. Previous left hydronephrosis has resolved, status post interval removal of prior left ureteral stent. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The lung bases demonstrate mild peripheral fibrotic changes. There is cardiomegaly. Particularly, the right atrium is markedly enlarged. There is no evidence for pneumoperitoneum or pneumatosis of the bowel. The CT angiogram demonstrates mild diffuse atherosclerotic changes of the aorta and its branches. The celiac, superior mesenteric, and inferior mesenteric arteries are all patent, without significant narrowing. There is mild bilateral narrowing at the origins of the renal arteries. There is a 16 mm saccular right common iliac artery aneurysm, which is partially thrombosed. There is also a focal dissection demonstrated within the right common iliac artery. The angiographic phase appearance of the liver, spleen, pancreas, and adrenals is unremarkable. There is a cortical defect associated with the upper pole of the left kidney, compatible with scarring. There is borderline dilation of small bowel loops scattered throughout the abdomen. In addition, there is fecal material seen within small bowel loops of the abdomen. Maximal transverse dimension approximates 3.1 cm. There is an abundance of stool seen within the colon. The colon is non-dilated. The bladder is unremarkable. There is degenerative disc disease of the lumbar spine. Degenerative joint disease is noted within the hips bilaterally. A Tarlov cyst is noted within the right lower sacrum. There is simple-appearing free fluid present within the perihepatic and peri-splenic. A small amount of free fluid is also noted within the deep pelvis. An upper mesenteric lymph node is enlarged at 12 mm (series 4, image # 171). IMPRESSION: 1. NO FINDINGS TO SUGGEST MESENTERIC ISCHEMIA IS NOTED IN THIS CT ANGIOGRAM OF THE ABDOMEN AND PELVIS. 2. MILDLY DILATED SMALL BOWEL LOOPS WITH INTRALUMINAL FECAL MATERIAL. ABUNDANCE OF STOOL WITHIN THE COLON. THE FINDINGS SUGGEST SOME DEGREE OF DISTAL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION, LIKELY DUE TO THE ABUNDANCE OF STOOL WITHIN THE COLON. LESS LIKELY, A PARTIAL SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION IS CONSIDERED. 3. A 16 MM RIGHT COMMON ILIAC ARTERY ANEURYSM WITH A SMALL FOCAL DISSECTION, AS ABOVE. 4. A SMALL AMOUNT OF ASCITES. FINDINGS WERE DISCUSSED WITH brooke dalton AT 0900 HOURS ON 9-19-2008. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The lung bases are clear and there are no pleural effusions. There are sternotomy wires in place with a calcified saphenous vein graph and LIMA graft. There is no pericardial fluid collection. There is a stable hypoattenuating lesion in segment 2 of the liver that is unchanged compared to prior exams consist with a benign finding. The gallbladder is normal in appearance. The common bile duct appears slightly prominent. There is no evidence of intrahepatic biliary dilatation. This finding is unchanged from previous exam and is of unlikely clinical significance. No obstructing stones or lesions are identified. The pancreas, spleen, adrenal glands, and kidneys are normal in appearance. The small bowel is normal in course and caliber. Again noted is a large diverticulum in the distal sigmoid colon with mucosal hyperemia, wall thickening, and surrounding inflammatory change of the pericolonic fat. The appearance is essentially unchanged from prior study. There is no focus of free air. Increased fluid is noted within the colon. There is no free fluid in the abdomen or pelvis. There is uncomplicated diverticulosis through the remainder of the descending colon and sigmoid colon. There are calcified mesenteric lymph nodes in the abdomen that are likely secondary to prior granulomatous infection. There are multiple regions of calcified plaque throughout the aorta and main arterial branches consistent with diffuse atherosclerotic disease. A focus of thrombus within the mid aorta that is unchanged as compared to prior studies (2; 170). The aorta at this level measures 2.4 cm in AP dimension. The bladder is normal in appearance. The prostate appear unremarkable. No suspicious bony lesions identified. There are degenerative changes of the spine. IMPRESSION: 1. A LARGE `` DIVERTICULUM `` OF THE SIGMOID COLON DEMONSTRATES MINIMAL SURROUNDING INFLAMMATORY CHANGE AND APPEARS STABLE COMPARED TO PRIOR EXAMINATION IN 2018-22-12. THIS FINDING IS OFTEN REFERRED TO AS A GIANT DIVERTICULUM. HOWEVER, THIS FINDING IS MOST CONSISTENT WITH A CHRONIC WALLED-OFF ABSCESS. THIS IS NOT AMENABLE TO DRAINAGE. 2. INCREASED FLUID IS NOTED IN THE COLON AND MAY REPRESENT AN ELEMENT OF COLITIS. 3. PRONOUNCED CALCIFIED ATHEROSCLEROTIC DISEASE OF THE ABDOMINAL AORTA AND MAIN ARTERIAL BRANCHES. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

Given stability of this lesion over 2 years, it is most consistent with a meningioma. Scattered periventricular and subcortical white matter signal intensities, which are nonspecific but presumably related to chronic small vessel ischemic changes, are stable. The ventricles and extra-axial fluid spaces are prominent in keeping with generalized volume loss. There is no acute intracranial hemorrhage nor infarction. Visualized orbital contents, paranasal sinuses, and mastoid air cells are preserved. Diffuse thickening of the calvarium is unchanged. Vascular flow voids at the base of the brain are patent. IMPRESSION: 1. Interval development of a small focus of GRE signal abnormality within the left posterior temporal lobe. No associated enhancement nor vasogenic edema. This lesion may be related to old microhemorrhage although small hemorrhagic metastasis is difficult to exclude. Recommend attention on follow-up imaging. 2. No acute intracranial abnormality. 3. Stable left posterior fossa meningioma. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: base of brain vascular flow voids (location:present)

An old micro hemorrhage or calcification is present within the right frontal lobe. No perfusion abnormality. The intracranial arterial vessels are patent with no filling defects, significant stenosis or aneurysm. IMPRESSION: 1. WHILE THIS MAY BE RELATED TO SEVERE CHRONIC SMALL VESSEL ISCHEMIC DISEASE, PONTINE AND EXTRAPONTINE MYELINOLYSIS COULD ALSO HAVE THIS APPEARANCE AND RECOMMEND CLINICAL CORRELATION WITH ANY RECENT ELECTROLYTE ABNORMALITIES. 2. RESULTS OF THIS STUDY WERE DISCUSSED WITH THE PHYSICIAN CARING OF THE PATIENT ON 2001/02/21. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is redemonstration of a anterior left temporal lobe cystic lesion with adjacent hyperintense T2 flair signal consistent with gliosis. The size, morphology, and signal characteristics of the lesion have not changed compared to 7-2-2003 examination. As before, there is no abnormal enhancement or soft tissue component associated with the lesion. There are a few scattered nonspecific foci of hyperintense T2 and flair signal in the white matter. Many of these were seen on prior study, however, some small punctate foci appear new from prior study, and prior lesions appear somewhat more prominent. This is likely related to technique and / or mild progression of these white matter changes compared to the 2001 study. The brain parenchyma is otherwise appropriate size, signal, and configuration. There is no mass, mass-effect, midline shift, hemorrhage, acute ischemia or ventriculomegaly. Dedicated thin section imaging through the temporal lobes demonstrate symmetric size, signal, and architecture in the hippocampi. Gray-white differentiation is appropriate throughout. There is no abnormal intra-or extra-axial enhancement. Orbital contents and surrounding soft tissues are within normal limits. Paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. IMPRESSION: 1. NO INTERVAL CHANGE IN LEFT TEMPORAL LOBE CYSTIC LESION AS DESCRIBED ABOVE. THE STABILITY AND LACK OF CONTRAST ENHANCEMENT IN THIS LESION ESSENTIALLY EXCLUDES NEOPLASM. THE APPEARANCE IS MOST CONSISTENT WITH SEQUELA OF PRIOR INJURY. 2. MILD INTERVAL INCREASE IN NONSPECIFIC NONENHANCING FOCI SIGNAL ABNORMALITY IN THE WHITE MATTER, LIKELY REFLECTING MILD CHRONIC SMALL VESSEL ISCHEMIC CHANGES. 3. NO ACUTE INTRACRANIAL ABNORMALITY. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: No acute intracranial hemorrhage is identified. No areas of a reduced diffusion are present to suggest recent ischemic injury. Scattered bilateral subcortical white matter signal abnormalities, which are nonspecific, are present. There is no discrete parenchymal mass or mass effect. Following intravenous administration of contrast, there are no areas of abnormal enhancement. The ventricles and extra-axial spaces are appropriate in size and relatively symmetric in configuration. The visualized portions of the paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. There is downward displacement of the cerebellar tonsils below the foramen magnum by approximately 11 mm. Additionally, there is mild sagging deformity of the brainstem with suggestion of decreased pontine-mammillary distance. No subdural effusions nor diffuse dural enhancement is appreciated. MRA images of the intracranial circulation demonstrate no significant flow-limiting stenosis nor cerebral aneurysm. There is fetal origin of the right posterior cerebral artery. No asymmetric perfusion of the brain is appreciated. IMPRESSION: 1. No acute infarct. 2. Downward displacement of the cerebellar tonsils below the foramen magnum by 11 mm. Question history of Chiari 1 malformation. Alternatively, the possibility of intracranial hypotension should be considered given features of mild sagging deformity of the brainstem although no subdural effusions nor dural enhancement is seen. 3. No steno-occlusive disease nor aneurysm of the vessels of the head or neck. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is no evidence of acute intracranial abnormality. There remain a few small foci of nonspecific nonenhancing white matter signal abnormality, unchanged from prior examination. There is a very small punctate focus of low GRE signal in the periventricular white matter of the left parietal lobe which is also unchanged from prior study and presumably represents a focus of prior microhemorrhage or mineralization. No large volume hemorrhage is seen. There is some mild intrinsic hyperintense T1 signal in the basal ganglia which may represent some physiologic mineralization or hepatic insufficiency. Following the administration of gadolinium there is an unchanged appearance of somewhat branching enhancement in the high left frontal lobe, unchanged from prior study and likely representing a DVA or capillary telangiectasia. No additional area of suspicious enhancement is seen. Bone marrow signal characteristics are appropriate. Surrounding soft tissues are within normal limits. Paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. There is a tiny mucus retention cyst or polyp in the medial aspect of the left maxillary, unchanged from prior study. Orbital contents are within normal limits. Basilar flow voids are intact. IMPRESSION: 1. NO ACUTE INTRACRANIAL ABNORMALITY. SPECIFICALLY, NO EVIDENCE OF INTRACRANIAL METASTATIC DISEASE. 2. STABLE APPEARANCE OF SMALL PUNCTATE FOCUS OF PRIOR MICROHEMORRHAGE OR MINERALIZATION IN THE LEFT PARIETAL LOBE. 3. STABLE NONSPECIFIC, NONENHANCING PUNCTATE FOCI OF WHITE MATTER SIGNAL ABNORMALITY. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: MR BRAIN: There has been interval right pterional craniotomy. An acute right temporal pole infarct is present possibly extending to involve the amygdala. Perfusion weighted imaging demonstrates no gross perfusion asymmetry. MR SPINE: Cord signal is normal throughout. No cord masses or flow voids or abnormal enhancement is seen in the cord or within the thecal sac. Minor degenerative changes are present at C5-6 and C6-7. A wedge compression fracture is present at L1 with mild loss in height and no enhancement suggesting chronicity. A central disc extrusion is present at L2-3 inferiorly. A fluid-fluid level is present at L5-S1 within the thecal sac. Note is made of dependent atelectasis and question of tiny bilateral pleural effusions. IMPRESSION: 1. BRAIN IMAGING DEMONSTRATES INTERVAL RIGHT TEMPORAL POLE INFARCT LIKELY INVOLVING THE AMYGDALA. NO OTHER RECENT OR OLD INFARCTS ARE IDENTIFIED AND NO ABNORMAL ENHANCEMENT IS IDENTIFIED. PERSISTENT SUBARACHNOID HEMORRHAGE IS PRESENT. 2. SPINE IMAGING DEMONSTRATES NO THECAL SAC VASCULAR MALFORMATIONS OR MASSES. CORD SIGNAL IS NORMAL. 3. AN L1 COMPRESSION FRACTURE IS LIKELY CHRONIC AND L2-3 CENTRAL DISC EXTRUSION IS PRESENT WITH NO SIGNIFICANT CANAL STENOSIS. A FLUID-FLUID LEVEL IS PRESENT IN THE MOST CAUDAL THECAL SAC, PRESUMABLY REPRESENTING A SMALL AMOUNT OF BLOOD. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: MR evaluation of the brain demonstrates stable postoperative changes following right frontal craniotomy. The resection cavity in the right frontal region is stable with associated surrounding hemosiderin staining and surrounding T2 parenchymal changes. While the cavity itself is stable in appearance, on post contrast imaging, a few surrounding foci of enhancement are identified. These measure in the 1 to 2-mm range and can be seen, for instance, on images 19 and 20 of series 12. The significance of these findings is uncertain. At present, the small size of the foci preclude further evaluation on perfusion imaging. On ASL and bolus perfusion imaging, there is expected perfusion abnormality in the resection cavity. No additional asymmetric perfusion abnormality is identified. There is no MR evidence of acute infarct. No acute intracranial blood products are identified. There is no new mass-effect, midline shift or downward herniation. Ventricular size is stable. Evaluation of the paranasal sinuses demonstrates no aggressive sinus disease. IMPRESSION: POSTOPERATIVE CHANGES FOLLOWING RIGHT FRONTAL CRANIOTOMY FOR TUMOR. WHILE THE RESECTION CAVITY ITSELF IS STABLE, A FEW SMALL (1-2 MM) FOCI OF ENHANCEMENT SURROUNDING THE CAVITY ARE NOW IDENTIFIED. THE SIGNIFICANCE OF THIS FINDING IS UNCERTAIN AND WHETHER IT REFLECTS TUMOR PROGRESSION OR RADIATION NECROSIS IS INDETERMINATE. ATTENTION ON FOLLOW-UP IMAGING IS RECOMMENDED. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: BRAIN: Infarcts in the left globus pallidus and subinsular region are stable. Other multiple foci of infarcts in the left ACA-MCA borderzone territories are stable to evolving. There are new foci of infarcts in the left posterior body of the corpus callosum, left posterior temporal lobe, left parietal lobe, and right occipital lobe. No hemorrhagic conversion is identified. Mild supratentorial white matter signal intensity abnormalities, which are nonspecific but presumably related to chronic small vessel ischemic changes, are present. No midline shift or brain herniation is identified. Ventricles are stable in size and configuration without hydrocephalus. Visualized orbital contents, paranasal sinuses, and mastoid air cells are preserved. Left ICA flow void is again noted to be diminutive. INTRACRANIAL ANGIOGRAM: Again demonstrated is occlusion of the left ICA with reconstitution of flow within the ACA and MCA through a patent ACOM and collaterals. PERFUSION: Extent and degree of Tmax delay within the left ACA and MCA territory are stable. ASL perfusion demonstrates high signal intensity within the left hemisphere suggestive of collateralization, unchanged. IMPRESSION: 1. EXPECTED EVOLUTION OF KNOWN INFARCTS IN THE LEFT HEMISPHERE WITH SOME NEW FOCI IN THE LEFT POSTERIOR CORPUS CALLOSUM, LEFT POSTERIOR TEMPORAL LOBE, LEFT PARIETAL LOBE, AND RIGHT OCCIPITAL LOBE, SOME OF WHICH MAY BE RELATED TO RECENT ANGIOGRAPHY. 2. NO HEMORRHAGIC CONVERSION. 3. STABLE ANGIOGRAPHIC FINDINGS WITH OCCLUSION OF THE LEFT INTERNAL CAROTID ARTERY AND RECONSTITUTION OF FLOW WITHIN THE ACA AND MCA THROUGH PATENT ACOM AND COLLATERALS. 4. STABLE EXTENT OF TMAX DELAY WITHIN THE LEFT CEREBRAL HEMISPHERE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

No evidence of recent ischemic injury. The signal intensity of the brain is preserved. There is no discrete parenchymal mass or mass effect. Following intravenous administration of contrast, there are no areas of abnormal enhancement. The ventricles and extra-axial spaces are appropriate in size and relatively symmetric in configuration. The visualized portions of the paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. No abnormality of the bony calvarium or orbits is identified. INTRACRANIAL ANGIOGRAM: There is no evidence of a flow-limiting stenosis, occlusion or aneurysm of the vessels comprising the circle of Willis. EXTRACRANIAL ANGIOGRAM: There is no evidence of a flow-limiting stenosis, occlusion, aneurysm, nor dissection of the neck vessels. PERFUSION: No asymmetric perfusion of the brain is appreciated. IMPRESSION: 1. Redemonstration of subarachnoid hemorrhage. 2. No vascular malformation or aneurysm of the vessels of the head or neck. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED. I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: BRAIN: MR imaging of the brain demonstrates small, nonspecific periventricular white matter foci of signal alteration. A few such foci are present in each frontal lobe, with one present in the left subinsular region. On sagittal imaging, a few more foci are evident in the frontoparietal operculum. Again, these findings are nonspecific and may be within normal limits for chronologic age. Otherwise, gray white matter differentiation is normal. Sulcal, cisternal, and ventricular size is within normal limits for chronologic age. There is no mass lesion, midline shift, or downward herniation. No abnormal postcontrast enhancement is identified. Diffusion imaging demonstrates no evidence of acute ischemia or infarction. CERVICAL SPINE: There is a normal cervical lordotic curvature. The anatomical relationship between the occiput, C1, and C2 is within normal limits. There are multiple foci of signal alteration in the cervical and thoracic vertebrae, likely reflecting hemangiomas. Otherwise, bone marrow signal intensity is normal. The MR appearance of the cervical spinal is normal. There are multilevel degenerative changes which are most pronounced at the C5-6 level where disk osteophyte complex, eccentric to the right results in a mild to moderate right neuroforaminal narrowing. THORACIC SPINE: There is a normal thoracic kyphotic curvature. Again, multiple foci of signal alteration in the thoracic vertebrae likely reflect hemangiomas. No concerning bone marrow signal alteration is identified. There is a small focus of increased signal in the thoracic cord apparent only on sagittal imaging at the inferior T12 level. This is not confirmed on axial imaging. No definite cord signal abnormality is identified. There is a right central disk protrusion at the T9-10 level which contacts the ventral surface of the cord, but does not result in cord deformation or signal change. No significant central or neuroforaminal narrowing is noted at the other thoracic level. IMPRESSION: 1. PUNCTATE FOCI OF FLAIR SIGNAL ABNORMALITY IN THE PERIVENTRICULAR WHITE MATTER. THIS IS NONSPECIFIC AND MAY BE NORMAL FOR CHRONOLOGIC AGE. NO ABNORMAL POSTCONTRAST ENHANCEMENT OR ACUTE INTRACRANIAL ABNORMALITY IS EVIDENT. 2. DEGENERATIVE CHANGES MOST PRONOUNCED AT C5-6 AND T9-10 AS DESCRIBED IN DETAIL ABOVE. NO DEFINITE CORD SIGNAL ABNORMALITY IS EVIDENT. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

instruction:
Your task is extracting medical entities from a single radiology report and returning them as a strictly defined relation graph in JSON format.

The output must be a SINGLE JSON ARRAY of relation objects following this structure:

```json
[
  {
    "anchor": "str (field:status)",
    "related": ["str (field:status)"]
  }
]
```

Where:

- "field" is one of: "observation", "location", "degree", "trend", "measurement"
- "status" is: 
  - "present", "absent", or "uncertain" for "observation", "location", "degree", "trend" fields.
  - "current" or "old" for "measurement" field.

Do NOT output anything other than JSON array.

GENERAL RULES

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.
If the report lacks Findings, use the given text.
Ignore History, Technique, Dose, Imaging Devices, Administrative Text, and Boilerplate Parts of the report.
Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: FINDINGS: MRI BRAIN: Stable post surgical changes within the sella. Stable rightward deviation of the pituitary infundibulum. No specific evidence of residual or recurrent suprasellar tumor. Bilateral intracranial flow voids are patent. No evidence of abnormal enhancement within the remainder of the brain. No mass effect or midline shift. Ventricular size is within normal limits. No evidence of abnormal extraaxial fluid collections. Sphenoid sinus mucosal thickening. Bilateral mastoid air cells are clear. MRI FULL SPINE: No evidence of abnormal vertebral body, paravertebral, epidural or interspinal enhancement to suggest metastatic disease. Alignment of the spine is within normal limits with no evidence of abnormal spinal cord signal. The vertebral heights are well maintained without evidence of acute vertebral body fracture. There has been progression of degenerative change, particularly at the left C3-4 level where there is a left paracentral disc protrusion. No evidence of significant central canal stenosis or neural foraminal stenosis. Left T5-6 paracentral small disc protrusion is not significantly changed. No evidence of cord compression. The remainder of the spine is without evidence of significant disc herniation, central canal stenosis or neural foraminal stenosis. Normal appearance of the conus and cauda equina with the conus terminating at approximately the L1-2 level. IMPRESSION: 1. STABLE POSTSURGICAL CHANGES WITHIN THE SELLA WITHOUT EVIDENCE OF RESIDUAL OR RECURRENT TUMOR. 2. NO EVIDENCE OF SPINAL METASTATIC DISEASE. 3. PROGRESSION OF CERVICAL SPINE DEGENERATIVE CHANGE COMPARED TO 2005, PARTICULARLY AT THE C3-4 LEVEL WHERE THERE IS A LEFT PARACENTRAL DISC PROTRUSION. NO EVIDENCE OF SIGNIFICANT CENTRAL CANAL OR NEURAL FORAMINAL STENOSIS. SUMMARY: 4 POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION. I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Postsurgical changes of left temporo-occipital parietal craniotomy for resection of a left occipital lobe mass are identified. A resection cavity is seen with surrounding T2 / FLAIR hyperintense signal in the adjacent white matter, with ex vacuo dilatation of the left occipital horn, which may communicate with the resection cavity. White matter signal abnormality without significant mass-effect is seen surrounding the left occipital horn with extension into the periventricular white matter in the left temporal lobe and parietal lobe. There is nodular enhancement along the left occipital horn and left temporal horn measuring up to 5.1 x 2.3 cm in aggregate (series 11, image 9). On perfusion imaging the largest area of periventricular enhancement seen along the mesial left temporal lobe exhibits increased cerebral blood volume. The brain is otherwise normal in signal and morphology and the ventricles are otherwise normal in size. No acute intracranial hemorrhage or infarct is identified. No midline shift is present. No abnormal extra-axial fluid collections are seen. Visualized orbital contents and mastoid air cells are preserved. Bilateral maxillary sinus mucous retention cysts are present. Vascular flow voids at the base of the brain are patent. Calvarium is intact. IMPRESSION: 1. POSTSURGICAL CHANGES OF LEFT OCCIPITAL LOBE TUMOR RESECTION WITH NODULAR ENHANCEMENT ALONG THE LEFT LATERAL VENTRICLE WHICH IS CONCERNING FOR RESIDUAL / RECURRENT DISEASE. IF THIS PATIENT HAS A HISTORY OF RADIATION THERAPY, THIS COULD ALTERNATIVELY REPRESENT RADIATION NECROSIS. COMPARISON WITH PRIOR EXAMINATIONS WOULD BE HELPFUL TO ASSESS FOR STABILITY OF THESE FINDINGS. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: periventricular white matter in left temporal lobe (location:present)
vascular flow voids at the base of the brain (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: No acute intracranial hemorrhage or infarct is identified. The ventricles and sulci are within normal limits. No parenchymal signal abnormality is identified. No obvious right retinal detachment or uveal enhancement is identified. MR angiography demonstrates a normal appearance of the visualized distal cervical, petrous, and cavernous internal carotid artery segments. There is a 1-2 mm triangular-shaped irregularity arising from the inferior aspect of the distal left supraclinoid, internal carotid artery segment, likely representing a prominent infundibulum, possibly of the anterior choroidal artery. An aneurysm is considered less likely. The right supraclinoid internal carotid artery segment is normal in appearance. The bilateral posterior communicating arteries are patent. The bilateral middle cerebral arteries, anterior cerebral arteries and anterior communicating artery are patent without focal stenosis or aneurysm. The distal vertebral arteries, vertebral basilar junction and bilateral posterior cerebral arteries are patent without focal stenosis or aneurysm. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF ACUTE INTRACRANIAL PATHOLOGY OR ABNORMAL ENHANCEMENT OR MASS LESION. 2. PATENT CIRCLE OF WILLIS WITHOUT MR ANGIOGRAPHIC FINDINGS TO SUGGEST CEREBRAL VASCULITIS. CEREBRAL ANGIOGRAPHY IS MORE SENSITIVE FOR EVALUATION OF VASCULITIS AND COULD BE PERFORMED AS CLINICALLY WARRANTED. 3. PROBABLE INFUNDIBULUM OF THE LEFT SUPRACLINOID CAROTID,? ANTERIOR CHOROIDAL ARTERY. SUMMARY: 4 POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION. I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: MRI brain: The coil mass is visualized at the anterior communicating artery aneurysm. There is a punctate focus measuring approximately 2 mm of increased signal on DWI sequence at the left caudate head with associated T2 hyperintensity on flair sequence (9:18). Midline structures are intact. Vascular flow voids at the base of the brain are preserved. No acute intracranial hemorrhage is identified. Visualized orbital contents, paranasal sinuses, and mastoid air cells are preserved. Calvarium is intact. MRA brain: There is flow related enhancement at the base of the aneurysm, which is consistent with recurrence and best seen on recent cerebral angiogram 2004/05/07. No high-grade stenosis or occlusion. No other aneurysm identified. MR brain perfusion: No evidence of delayed Tmax. IMPRESSION: 1. No major vascular territory infarct or acute intracranial hemorrhage. Punctate infarct in the left caudate head, likely embolic. 2. Coil mass at the anterior communicated artery aneurysm with similar recurrence at the aneurysm base compared to recent angiogram. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is redemonstration of postsurgical changes related to the patient's prior multiple right frontal oligodendroglioma resection. Along the posterior margin of the resection cavity there are two nonenhancing nodules that have increased in size compared to prior examination (see series 3 image 16, series 6 image 22 and series 6 image 24). One nodule now measures approximately 14 x 9 x 20 mm and the other nodule measures approximately 14 x 12 x 17 mm. A stable nonspecific area of parenchymal signal intensity abnormality is noted along the most superior aspect of the posterior surgical cavity margin (series 6 image 27). Ex vacuo dilatation of the right lateral ventricle is again noted. Otherwise, the ventricles and sulci are preserved, without ventriculomegaly. Old punctate small right intraventricular blood products are noted. There is no acute intracranial hemorrhage, midline shift, or acute infarction. There are no abnormal intra-axial or extra-axial fluid collections. There is no abnormal enhancement on postcontrast images. The orbits, mastoid air cells, flow-voids at the skull base, and cervicomedullary junction are preserved. Symmetric brain perfusion is shown without evidence of focal perfusion abnormality. Perfusion defects expected secondary to the patient's known post surgical changes are seen. Otherwise, no focal perfusion asymmetries are identified. IMPRESSION: 1. INTERVAL INCREASE IN SIZE OF TWO NODULES ALONG THE POSTERIOR MARGIN OF THE SURGICAL RESECTION CAVITY CONCERNING FOR TUMOR RECURRENCE. 2. POST SURGICAL CHANGES RELATED TO PATIENT'S PRIOR MULTIPLE OLIGODENDROGLIOMA RESECTIONS. 3. FINDINGS WERE DISCUSSED WITH THE ORDERING CLINICIAN (Wyatt MD). SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is no definite acute infarction, hemorrhage, or hydrocephalus. Cisterns and sulci are grossly maintained. Posterior fossa demonstrates no discrete mass signal alteration or space-occupying abnormality. The suspected enhancing abnormality related to the right petrous apex and dura is most likely vascular in nature. There is interval development of a T2 hyperintense, 5 mm lesion, which is in the right parietal region near the singulum, immediately to the right of midline and possibly sulcal reversed peripheral cortical in location. This is best seen on series 5 image 21 and series 3 image 12. Postcontrast images demonstrate marginal enhancement (series 10 image 21). There may be a fluid debris level best seen on axial T2. There are no definite associated hemorrhagic contents. Again noted are nonspecific T2 hyperintense lesions measuring approximately 5 mm in diameter in the parotid glands. IMPRESSION: 1. NO DEFINITE MASS OR ENHANCING ABNORMALITY RELATED TO THE RIGHT PETROUS BONE OR DURA IN THE MIDDLE CRANIAL FOSSA OR POSTERIOR FOSSA. 2. INTERVAL DEVELOPMENT OF A CYSTIC lesion WITH possible FLUID-DEBRIS level IN THE right PARIETAL LOBE WITH peripheral enhanceMENT. THIS MAY BE peripheral CORTICAL VERSE SULCAL, AND IS OF UNCERTAIN etiology. THE findingS WOULD BE MOST compatible WITH AN inflammatory OR INFECTIOUS PROCESS, AND correlation WITH CSF MAY BE OF VALUE TO CLEAR THE possibility OF meningitis. THE appearANCE IS HIGHLY UNCHARACTERISTIC FOR metastatic disease, which WOULD BE A FAR LESS ALTERNATE CONSIDERATION. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION findings discussed with Dougherty MD, 5/6 / 2016 at 1600 hours. I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: BRAIN: Most of the numerous previously seen supratentorial punctate foci of enhancement are again seen, but are less conspicuous on the present study. Some are no longer evident. The pial enhancement seen in the superior cerebellum remains but is similarly less conspicuous. No acute intracranial hemorrhage is identified. No areas of a reduced diffusion are present to suggest recent ischemic injury. The ventricles and extra-axial spaces are appropriate in size and relatively symmetric in configuration. Bony orbits and their soft tissue contents are symmetric and intact. No abnormality of the bony calvarium is identified. The visualized portions of the paranasal sinuses are clear. Bilateral mastoid effusions are present. Tiny right parietal intrinsically T1 bright nodule is likely a scalp lipoma. IMPRESSION: 1. Overall, interval response to therapy with decreased prominence of the previously seen foci of supratentorial enhancement, some of which are no longer seen at all. The leptomeningeal enhancement over the cerebellum is similarly less conspicuous. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The study demonstrates no acute intracranial hemorrhage or infarct. There are stable non-enhancing white matter lesions in the periventricular and pericallosal white matter and bilateral middle cerebellar peduncles. There is interval development of a small punctate enhancing lesion in the right temporal lobe white matter which could represent an enhancing demyelinating lesion. There are stable post-surgical changes associated with bilateral pterional and left frontal craniotomy with stable encephalomalacia of the left frontal lobe. There is significant interval increase in ventricular size concerning for communicating hydrocephalus. MR brain perfusion demonstrates no perfusion abnormality. MR angiogram demonstrates metallic artifact from multiple surgical clips and left SCA and left P1 endovascular coils. There is a stable 7 mm residual aneurysm at the proximal left superior cerebellar artery. There is a stable left cavernous / paraclinoid ICA aneurysm measuring 3 mm in size and projecting posteroinferiorly. IMPRESSION: 1. SIGNIFICANT INTERVAL INCREASE IN VENTRICULAR SIZE, CONCERNING FOR COMMUNICATING HYDROCEPHALUS. 2. STABLE 3 MM LEFT CAVERNOUS / PARACLINOID ICA ANEURYSM. STABLE 7 MM RESIDUAL ANEURYSM AT THE PROXIMAL LEFT SUPERIOR CEREBELLAR ARTERY. 3. STABLE MULTIPLE PERIVENTRICULAR, WHITE MATTER, AND BILATERAL MIDDLE CEREBELLAR PEDUNCLE WHITE MATTER LESIONS. THERE IS INTERVAL DEVELOPMENT OF A SMALL PUNCTATE ENHANCING LESION AT THE RIGHT TEMPORAL PERIVENTRICULAR WHITE MATTER WHICH COULD REPRESENT A NEW ENHANCING DEMYELINATING LESION. NO ACUTE INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE OR INFARCT. 4. THE FINDINGS WERE DISCUSSED WITH CASEY ANDRES'S NURSE PRACTITIONER, Dillon Ali MD, AT 1100 HOURS ON september 26th, 2007. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is no intra-cranial hemorrhage, edema, mass, mass effect, shift of midline, or infarct. Scattered periventricular and subcortical white matter signal intensity abnormalities most likely reflect chronic small vessel ischemic disease. The ventricles and sulci are appropriate in size and configuration. On MRA, a coil mass is again seen within a previously coiled basilar tip aneurysm sac. Compared to 04/05/2009, there appears to be an increase in flow related enhancement within the basilar tip coil mass, and coil compaction or recanalization is not excluded. There is a stent in the left P1 segment of the posterior cerebral artery, which obscures flow related enhancement. The right posterior cerebral artery is patent. There continues to be irregularity of the cavernous segments of both internal carotid arteries, presumably representing atherosclerosis. There are two focal outpouchings seen along the distal left internal carotid artery, measuring approximately 1--2 mm, which may reflect small infundibula or aneurysms. Additionally, there is a small focal outpouching off the right ICA near the origin of the right PComA, which also may affect an infundibulum or small aneurysm. These appear unchanged compared to the angiogram of 2-3-2009. Partially imaged are multilevel degenerative changes of the upper cervical spine. IMPRESSION: 1. BASILAR TIP ANEURYSM COIL MASS, WITH INCREASED FLOW RELATED ENHANCEMENT SEEN INFERIOR TO THE COIL MASS COMPARED TO 3-26-09. COIL COMPACTION AND / OR RECANALIZATION IS NOT ENTIRELY EXCLUDED, AND MAY NEED TO BE CORRELATED WITH CATHETER ANGIOGRAPHY. 2. IRREGULARITY OF THE CAVERNOUS INTERNAL CAROTID ARTERIES BILATERALLY, PRESUMABLY REFLECTING ATHEROSCLEROTIC DISEASE. 3. SMALL FOCAL OUTPOUCHINGS ARISING FROM THE DISTAL INTERNAL CAROTID ARTERIES BILATERALLY, WHICH MAY REFLECT EITHER INFUNDIBULA OR TINY ANEURYSMS, AND APPEAR UNCHANGED COMPARED TO THE ANGIOGRAM OF 03/09/09.. THESE FINDINGS WERE DISCUSSED BY TELEPHONE WITH Lester MD, ON 01/31/10 AT 11 A. M. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: left P1 segment of posterior cerebral artery (location:present)
both cavernous segments of internal carotid arteries (location:present)
right ICA near origin of right PComA (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: Postsurgical changes are present related to right temporoparietal craniotomy with a resection cavity in the right temporal lobe. Small residual amount of gas and extra-axial fluid are identified around the resection cavity. Following administration of intravenous contrast, there is enhancement around the resection cavity with the greatest nodular component along the posterior margin. Nodular enhancement is noted to abuts the atrium of the right lateral ventricle. Additional nonenhancing signal abnormality involves the right temporal lobe, right parietal lobe, and right parietal periventricular white matter. Perfusion imaging demonstrates expected decreased perfusion within the resection cavity. Elsewhere, no enhancing lesions are identified. No significant midline shift or herniation is seen. There is ex vacuo dilatation of the occipital horn of the right lateral ventricle. Otherwise, the ventricles are appropriate in size. Mild hemosiderin deposition is noted around the resection cavity with no evidence of hemorrhage or infarct elsewhere. The orbits and retrobulbar structures are symmetric. The paranasal sinuses are clear. A small right mastoid effusion is identified. Flow-voids at the base of skull are maintained. IMPRESSION: POSTSURGICAL CHANGES RELATED TO RIGHT TEMPORAL LOBE RESECTION WITH NODULAR ENHANCEMENT SURROUNDING THE RESECTION CAVITY AND A GREATER AREA OF NONENHANCING SIGNAL ABNORMALITY INVOLVING THE RIGHT TEMPORAL LOBE, PARIETAL LOBE, AND PERIVENTRICULAR WHITE MATTER. IN ABSENCE OF PRIOR IMAGING, IT IS UNCLEAR TO WHICH EXTENT THESE FINDINGS MAY REPRESENT POSTTREATMENT CHANGE VERSUS RESIDUAL TUMOR. RECOMMEND COMPARISON WITH PRIOR IMAGING. IF PRIOR IMAGING IS NOT AVAILABLE, THIS EXAMINATION WILL SERVE AS THE PATIENT'S POST-TREATMENT BASELINE FOR FUTURE COMPARISONS. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: occipital horn of right lateral ventricle (location:present)
