input: FINDINGS: The study demonstrates interval development of new bilateral caudate bodies and left occipital lobe infarct. There is also interval increase in right watershed area and bilateral frontal, right more than left, ACA territory acute infarction, superimposed on area of subacute infarction in bilateral frontal lobe and insular cortices with subacute gyral contrast enhancement. There is stable diffuse bilateral cerebral convexity subarachnoid hemorrhage and small intraventricular hemorrhage. The ventricles are stable in size with presence of a right frontal approach ventricular catheter, tip of the catheter is in the right ventricular frontal horn. MR angiogram demonstrates stable left supraclinoid ICA stenosis and irregularity of bilateral anterior cerebral arteries. Vasospasm could not be entirely excluded. There is metallic susceptibility artifact in the area of right supraclinoid ICA. The posterior circulation is within normal limits. MR brain perfusion demonstrates delayed perfusion in bilateral watershed area and right frontal lobe, worsening compared to prior MRI on 3/16/2000. IMPRESSION: 1. INTERVAL DEVELOPMENT OF NEW ACUTE INFARCT IN BILATERAL CAUDATE BODIES, LEFT OCCIPITAL LOBE, RIGHT WATERSHED AREA AND BILATERAL FRONTAL LOBE, RIGHT MORE THAN LEFT; IN THE BILATERAL ANTERIOR CEREBRAL ARTERY TERRITORY. 2. STABLE DIFFUSE CEREBRAL CONVEXITY SUBARACHNOID HEMORRHAGE, SMALL INTRAVENTRICULAR HEMORRHAGE. THE VENTRICLES ARE STABLE IN SIZE. 3. THERE IS GYRAL CONTRAST ENHANCEMENT IN THE AREAS OF SUBACUTE INFARCT INVOLVING THE BILATERAL FRONTAL AND INSULAR CORTICES. 4. MR ANGIOGRAM DEMONSTRATES IRREGULARITY OF BILATERAL ANTERIOR CEREBRAL ARTERIES, CONCERNING FOR VASOSPASM. 5. MR PERFUSION DEMONSTRATES DELAYED PERFUSION IN BILATERAL WATERSHED AREAS AND RIGHT FRONTAL LOBE, WORSENING COMPARED TO PRIOR MRI FROM March 16, 2000. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION. I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: MR BRAIN: Comparing with the prior examination, there is progressive right more than left cerebellar white matter abnormalities, particularly involving the right lateral cerebellar hemisphere. White matter abnormalities involving the midline pons is smaller, as well as the right lateral pons, and the left pontine lesion is larger. There are persistent innumerable lesions, and the images are non-coplanar on comparison. Nevertheless, there is a new right dorsal putaminal lesion (series 8 image 16), and the left posterior occipital horn periventricular lesion (series 8 image 17). Numerous new T2 lesions are noted in the bilateral centrum semi-ovale. Many of these lesions are T1 hypointense. Enhancing lesions include the dorsal right putaminal lesion (series 12 image 16), left occipital parietal region (series 12 image 17), faint right periatrial region (series 12 image 12, 14-15), left frontal subcortical U fiber region, right anterior temporal lobe, left periventricular region (series 12 images 21 through 22), and left frontal region (series 12 image 24). No acute intracranial hemorrhage or acute infarct. No mass effect or midline shift. Ventricular size is within normal limits. No abnormal extra-axial fluid collections. No abnormal enhancement is seen. Visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. MR PERFUSION: Symmetric brain perfusion is shown without evidence of focal perfusion abnormality. IMPRESSION: 1. AS DISCUSSED ABOVE, MULTIPLE NEW T2 HYPERINTENSE AND ENHANCING LESIONS OF THE BRAIN. 2. FINDINGS ARE CONSISTENT WITH PROGRESSIVE, ACTIVE DEMYELINATION. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: left frontal subcortical U fiber region (location:present)

input: FINDINGS: The study demonstrates no acute intracranial hemorrhage or infarct. No focal mass or abnormal intraparenchymal enhancement identified. There is diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement secondary to extensive leptomeningeal collaterals. The ventricles, bilateral orbital contents, paranasal sinuses, and mastoid air cells are within normal limits. MR angiogram demonstrates no significant interval change in bilateral supraclinoid internal carotid arteries, bilateral MCA, and ACA stenosis with extensive basal and leptomeningeal collaterals. There is stable enlargement of bilateral middle meningeal arteries. There is also stable steno-occlusive disease of the right posterior cerebral artery. Compared to prior study, there is interval worsening of steno-occlusive disease of the left posterior cerebral artery with interval development of multifocal stenosis. There is stable caliber of the basilar artery and bilateral intradural vertebral arteries. MR perfusion demonstrates delayed perfusion in bilateral ACAs, right anterior MCA, bilateral watershed areas, and left posterior cerebral artery territory. No evidence of decreasing CBV, indicating good collateral flows. Bilateral STA-MCA bypasses are patent, left greater than right. IMPRESSION: 1. NO ACUTE INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE OR INFARCT. 2. STABLE OCCLUSION OF BILATERAL SUPRACLINOID INTERNAL CAROTID ARTERIES, BILATERAL MCA, BILATERAL ACA, WITH EXTENSIVE BASAL AND LEPTOMENINGEAL COLLATERALS. THERE IS ALSO STABLE STENO-OCCLUSIVE DISEASE OF THE RIGHT POSTERIOR CEREBRAL ARTERY. 3. INTERVAL WORSENING OF STENO-OCCLUSIVE DISEASE OF THE LEFT POSTERIOR CEREBRAL ARTERY WITH INTERVAL DEVELOPMENT OF MULTIFOCAL STENOSIS. THERE IS CORRESPONDING DELAYED PERFUSION IN THE LEFT PCA TERRITORY ON THE MR PERFUSION. 4. MR PERFUSION DEMONSTRATES DELAYED PERFUSION, WITHOUT DECREASE IN CBV, IN BILATERAL ACA TERRITORIES, RIGHT ANTERIOR MCA TERRITORY, LEFT PCA TERRITORY, AND BILATERAL WATERSHED AREA. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There are numerous foci of GRE hypointense signal within the bilateral cerebellar hemispheres compatible with cavernous malformations, which appear stable from the previous examination. Foci of GRE hypointensity also representing cavernous malformations are seen within the left pontomedullary junction junction, left pons, right upper pons, left subinsular region, and left precentral gyrus are stable. A hemosiderin lined cleft within the left subinsular region compatible with remote intraparenchymal hemorrhage is seen. A stable area of T2 / FLAIR hyperintense signal within the left parietal lobe is seen in involving cortex and subcortical white matter, measuring 2.7 x 2.7 cm in the axial plane. The cortex in this region appears thickened, and there is no volume loss. This area does not enhance. The ventricles are stable, with stable mild ex vacuo dilatation of the left lateral ventricle. Expected flow voids at the base of the brain are patent. The globes and orbits are preserved. The paranasal sinuses and mastoid vessels are clear. The calvarium is intact. IMPRESSION: 1. STABLE APPEARANCE OF INNUMERABLE PRESUMED CAVERNOUS MALFORMATIONS WITHIN THE POSTERIOR FOSSA AND SCATTERED CAVERNOUS MALFORMATIONS IN THE SUPRATENTORIAL COMPARTMENT. 2. STABLE APPEARANCE OF OLD HEMORRHAGIC CAVITY IN THE REGION OF THE LEFT EXTERNAL CAPSULE. 3. AGAIN DEMONSTRATED IS A FOCAL AREA OF NON-ENHANCING T2 / FLAIR HYPERINTENSE SIGNAL WITHIN THE LEFT PARIETAL CORTEX WITH ASSOCIATED CORTICAL THICKENING, STABLE DATING BACK TO 5-28-1998. APPEARANCE OF CORTICAL THICKENING AND LACK OF FOCAL VOLUME LOSS (E. G. SULCAL PROMINENCE) IN THIS REGION IS UNUSUAL FOR ENCEPHALOMALACIA. CONSIDERATIONS INCLUDE DYSPLASIA OR LOW GRADE NEOPLASIA. 4. NO ACUTE INTRACRANIAL ABNORMALITY. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

There is mild diffuse enlargement of the ventricles and sulci which may reflect mild parenchymal global volume loss. There is no pathologic enhancement. The orbits are unremarkable. The visualized paranasal sinuses appear clear. MRA of the vessels compromising the circle of Willis appear patent. There are no areas of significant stenosis and no aneurysmal dilatation. ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations.

input: FINDINGS: Brain: The brain parenchyma demonstrates no abnormal signal intensity. No acute intracranial hemorrhage or infarct is identified. No focal mass, mass effect, or midline shift is present. On post-gadolinium images, there are no abnormal areas of enhancement. Ventricles are symmetric in size and configuration without ventriculomegaly. Visualized orbital contents and mastoid air cells are present. Mild thickening in the ethmoid sinuses. Air-fluid levels are present in the left maxillary and the right sphenoid sinuses. Retention cyst or polyp in the right maxillary sinus. Vascular flow voids at the base of the brain are patent. Calvarium is intact. Cervical / thoracic spine: No abnormal enhancement is seen either in the thoracic or cervical cord. As seen on the study from yesterday, mild multi-level degenerative disk disease are present in the cervical spine. The only additional information obtained on today's study is mild enhancement and increased vascularity associated with the left paracentral disk protrusion. The disk protrusion demonstrates mild increased T2 signal intensity. These finding suggests the acute nature of the disk protrusion. Mild central canal stenosis at C5-C6 with mild left neural foramina narrowing. Remainder levels without significant canal or foramina stenosis. IMPRESSION: 1. NO ACUTE INTRACRANIAL ABNORMALITY. 2. PARANASAL SINUS MUCOSAL THICKENING WITH AIR-FLUID LEVELS IN THE LEFT MAXILLARY AND RIGHT SPHENOID SINUSES. PLEASE CORRELATE CLINICALLY FOR ACUTE SINUSITIS. 3. MILD ENHANCEMENT AND INCREASED SIGNAL ASSOCIATED WITH THE LEFT PARACENTRAL DISK PROTRUSION AT C5-C6 LIKELY INDICATING THE ACUTE NATURE OF THE DISK PROTRUSION. 4. NO ABNORMAL ENHANCEMENT IN THE THORACIC OR CERVICAL CORD. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: MRI CERVICAL SPINE WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST: There is reversal of the cervical spine's lordosis in the upper cervical spine. There is preservation of the vertebral body height and intervertebral disc except at C7-T1 which is not segmented. There is no cervical spinal cord signal abnormality. There is redemonstration of a ventral epidural hematoma extending from the level of T1 inferiorly. No signal abnormality is seen in the cervical vertebral bodies or the cervical spinal cord. MRI BRAIN: There is redemonstration of infarction involving the right cerebellar hemisphere, left medioccipital lobe, left medioccipital parietal and left subinsular regions; similar in comparison to the prior study. There has been interval increase in extent of the restricted diffusion involving the left paramedian parietal lobe and restricted diffusion in bilateral watershed areas of the cerebral hemispheres is more evident in comparison to the prior study. There is developing dural enhancement in the infarcted tissues. There is redemonstration of diffusely prominent extraaxial spaces; stable. There is a small right posterior fossa subdural hematoma; stable. MRA of the circle of Willis demonstrates irregularities in the anterior and posterior circulation which is most prominent in the vertebral basilar cistern and in the bilateral MCA arteries that is better demonstrated on the angiogram of 12/16/2010. There is redemonstration of siderosis outlining the brain stem, small amount of layering blood in bilateral occipital horns, and small amount of subarachnoid hemorrhage overlying the cerebral convexities; stable. Bolus perfusion weighted imaging demonstrates prolonged mean transit time predominately in the posterior circulation, left more than right. IMPRESSION: 1. REDEMONSTRATION OF STABLE INFARCTIONS INVOLVING THE INFRATENTORIAL AND SUPRATENTORIAL BRAIN WITH INTERVAL OCCURRENCE / PROGRESSION OF LEFT PARAMEDIAN PARIETAL INFARCTION AND INCREASE CONSPICUITY OF BILATERAL WATERSHED SUPRATENTORIAL INFARCTIONS. 2. ANTERIOR AND POSTERIOR IMAGES OF THE BRAIN DEMONSTRATE IRREGULAR WALLS WHICH ARE MORE PROMINENT IN THE POSTERIOR CIRCULATION AND IN THE MCA ARTERIES. FINDINGS ARE SUGGESTIVE OF A VASCULOPATHY / VASCULITIS OR POSSIBLY VASOSPASM. 3. REDEMONSTRATION OF STABLE FINDINGS OF SUBARACHNOID AND INTRAVENTRICULAR HEMORRHAGE. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Again seen are postsurgical changes related to right parietal craniotomy and resection of tumor. There is a stable small extra-axial fluid collection along this surgical site without significant change since prior exam. There is stable to slightly minimally increased area of enhancement along the anterior aspect of the resection margin (series 13, images 19 and 20). In addition, there is slight increase in diffusion signal abnormality along the right posterior frontal lobe anterior and superior to the resection cavity. Bilateral confluent white matter FLAIR signal changes are also again seen which are slightly more extensive in both the anterior and posterior direction. There is no midline shift, intracranial hemorrhage, or ventriculomegaly. The sinuses appear clear. The basal cisterns are patent. The vascular flow voids at the skull base are preserved. IMPRESSION: 1. STABLE TO MINIMALLY INCREASED AREA OF ENHANCEMENT ANTERIOR TO THE RESECTION MARGIN AS DESCRIBED ABOVE. THERE IS SLIGHTLY INCREASED DIFFUSION SIGNAL ABNORMALITY ANTERIOR AND SUPERIOR TO THE RESECTION MARGIN COMPARED TO THE PRIOR EXAM. BILATERAL CONFLUENT FLAIR SIGNAL CHANGES ARE ALSO SLIGHTLY MORE EXTENSIVE IN BOTH THE ANTERIOR AND POSTERIOR DIRECTIONS. THESE FINDINGS MAY AGAIN BE RELATED TO TREATMENT RELATED CHANGES AND / OR PROGRESSION OF DISEASE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is decreased edema surrounding the numerous supratentorial metastatic enhancing brain lesions. There are no new lesions. Further decreased size of most lesions is noted compared to the 18/10/27 study. The prior largest index lesion in the para-sagittal right frontal lobe measures 7 x 8 mm, previously 10 x 10 mm (series 10, image 23). There is new hemorrhage within the right temporal metastatic lesion seen on series 10, image 13, which may be treatment related. Otherwise the hemorrhagic lesions appear unchanged. The lesion in the right parietal lobe is unchanged and may represent a metastatic lesion with eccentric hemorrhage or a metastatic lesion adjacent to a cavernous malformation. There is no restricted diffusion. There is no significant edema and there is no midline shift. Ventricles are normal in size. The para-nasal sinuses and orbits are normal. Vascular flow voids are normal. Calvarium and midline structures are normal. Bilateral mastoid effusions are present. There is a 1 cm focus of enhancement within the right parasagittal calvarial vertex seen on series 10, image 29, that appears relatively similar in size compared to the OCTOBER 27, 2018 study. A prominent arachnoid granulation versus bony metastatic disease remains in the differential. IMPRESSION: 1. DECREASED SIZE OF MULTIPLE METASTATIC LESIONS. NO NEW LESIONS IDENTIFIED. 2. STABLE 1 CM SIGNAL ABNORMALITY WITHIN THE RIGHT PARASAGITTAL CALVARIAL VERTEX. DIFFERENTIAL INCLUDES A PROMINENT ARACHNOID GRANULATION VERSUS BONY METASTASIS. RECOMMEND INCLUSION OF THE ENTIRE CALVARIUM ON THE NEXT FOLLOW-UP SHORT-TERM PET SCAN VERSUS BONE SCAN TO HELP FURTHER DIFFERENTIATE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

Interval right frontotemporal post-surgical biopsy change including biopsy tract is noted. Heterogeneous complex cystic right temporal lobe lesion is not significantly changed in size but does demonstrate slightly decreased areas of edema. Areas of restricted diffusion are not significantly changed within the right posterior temporal lobe lesion. Right posterior temporal lobe biopsy tract is again noted. Associated decreased mass effect on the right lateral ventricle is noted. No evidence of midline shift. No evidence of brain herniation. Left frontal lobe encephalomalacia related to a remote injury is stable in appearance. Visualized paranasal sinuses are clear. Small bilateral mastoid effusions. IMPRESSION: 1. DECREASED SIZE OF RIGHT PUTAMEN / CORONA RADIATA BRAIN ABSCESS WITH DECREASED AMOUNT VASOGENIC EDEMA AND MASS EFFECT. 2. STABLE SIZE OF RIGHT TEMPORAL LOBE ABSCESS WITH SLIGHTLY DECREASED ASSOCIATED EDEMA. 3. RESTRICTED DIFFUSION NOTED WITHIN THE RIGHT LATERAL VENTRICLE REPRESENTING VENTRICULITIS WHICH IS NEW COMPARED TO PRIOR EXAMINATION. 4. INTERVAL BIOPSY CHANGES IN THE RIGHT FRONTOTEMPORAL REGION AND STABLE BIOPSY CHANGES OF THE RIGHT POSTERIOR TEMPORAL LOBE. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: FINDINGS: MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST: There are multiple periventricular and subcortical signal abnormalities in the supratentorial and infratentorial brain (including the medulla, midbrain, pons and bilateral cerebellar hemispheres). None of these abnormalities demonstrate enhancement. There is no acute infarction, intracranial hemorrhage, intra-axial / extra-axial mass, extra-axial collections. Orbits are grossly unremarkable. The visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. MRI CERVICAL SPINE WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST: There is normal alignment of the cervical vertebral bodies with preservation of the vertebral bodies and intervertebral disc heights. Bone marrow signal is within normal limits. There is a broad-based disc bulge associated with a large central disc protrusion at the level of C3-C4 causing deformity of the central spinal cord and resulting in moderate vertebral canal stenosis. There is no significant neural foraminal stenosis. There are two well-defined foci of T2 signal abnormality in the cervical spine the first located at the level of C3-C4 and the second at the level of C6; there may be additional multiple foci of T2 signal abnormality in the spinal cord which are not well evaluated on this study secondary to artifacts on the axial T2 sequence of the spine. No definite enhancement is seen in the spinal cord. There is no intradural or extradural mass or abnormal enhancement. IMPRESSION: MULTIPLE SIGNAL ABNORMALITIES IN THE SUPRATENTORIAL AND INFRATENTORIAL BRAIN. AT LEAST TWO WELL-DEFINED SIGNAL ABNORMALITIES IN THE CERVICAL SPINE WITH POSSIBILITY OF OTHER ADDITIONAL ABNORMALITIES WHICH ARE NOT WELL DEFINED ON THIS STUDY. CONSTELLATION OF FINDINGS WOULD BE SUGGESTIVE OF A DEMYELINATING PROCESS IN THE APPROPRIATE CLINICAL SETTING. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Pulmonary arteries: The pulmonary arterial system is well opacified and exhibits no intraluminal filling defect to suggest pulmonary embolism. Pulmonary artery is normal in size. Neck: Normal thyroid. Heart: Normal in size with no pericardial effusion. Mediastinum: The aorta and great vessels are normal in course and caliber. There is atherosclerotic calcifications of the aorta, with some coronary artery atherosclerotic calcifications. The esophagus and gastroesophageal junction are normal in appearance. Lymph nodes: No enlarged mediastinal, hilar, supraclavicular or axillary lymph nodes. Lungs: Small bilateral pleural effusions. No focal consolidation. No suspicious pulmonary nodules or masses. Musculoskeletal: No aggressive osseous lesion is identified in the visualized bones. Upper abdomen: The study was not done for evaluation of his liver neoplasms. Again identified hypodensity in anterior leads in the left of the liver from prior chemo embolization there is evidence of cirrhosis of the liver, increasing ascites and to assess. Gallbladder again showing an enhancing mass adjacent to the liver. Metallic density is seen in, and the right upper quadrant could be embolization coils. Addendum Begins Multiplanar reformations and maximum intensity projections were performed on an independent 3D workstation and corroborate the findings. Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. PATIENT WITH KNOWN HISTORY OF MULTIFOCAL HCC AND PRIOR CHEMO AND RADIOEMBOLIZATION THERAPY. 3. HEPATIC CIRRHOSIS WITH INCREASING ASCITES, SPLENOMEGALY AND VARICES. 4. AGAIN SEEN IS AN IRREGULAR MASS WITH ENHANCEMENT OF THE GALLBLADDER WALL, CONCERNING FOR PRIMARY OR METASTATIC CANCER. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The heart size is normal without a pericardial effusion. The aorta is normal in course and caliber with a standard left-sided 3-vessel aortic arch. There are no significant atherosclerotic calcifications of the aorta or coronary arteries. The pulmonary arterial tree is well opacified, without filling defects to suggest pulmonary embolism. The main pulmonary artery is normal in course and caliber. The lungs appear clear without focal consolidation. Scattered densely calcified granuloma are seen throughout the lung parenchyma, likely related to prior granulomatous disease. No pleural effusions. The airways are patent and of normal course and caliber. No mediastinal, hilar or axillary lymphadenopathy. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen is unremarkable. No suspicious bony lesions identified. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. CLEAR LUNGS; NO CT FINDINGS TO ACCOUNT FOR THE PATIENT'S CHEST PAIN. SUMMARY: 1-NO SIGNIFICANT ABNORMALITY I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There are no filling defects within the pulmonary arteries to suggest embolism. The heart is markedly enlarged, particularly with left atrial dilatation, in keeping with known history of mitral regurgitation. There is moderate atherosclerotic calcification at the aortic arch. There is conventional arch anatomy. The thyroid gland appears normal. There are several enlarged lymph nodes seen throughout the mediastinum. For example a right paratracheal lymph node measures 1.4 cm in short axis (series 3, image 264). An additional right paratracheal lymph node measures 1.5 cm (series 3 and image 247). Prevascular lymph nodes measure up to 1.2 cm (series 3, image 250). An AP window lymph node measures 1.1 cm (series 3, image 249). There is no hilar or axillar lymphadenopathy. There is an interval increase of marked diffuse interlobular and intralobular septal thickening compared to prior CT of the abdomen from February 2002. Addition, there are large geographic groundglass opacities throughout both lungs, also increased compared to prior study. The findings are most compatible with diffuse brain edema superimposed on chronic lung changes. There are small bilateral pleural effusions, which are new compared to prior CT. There is no pneumothorax. There is no discrete lung consolidation. The limited images of the upper abdomen again demonstrate a 2.2-cm low-attenuation right adrenal nodule which was previous to characterize as a probable myelolipoma. Please see report of CT abdomen from 3-12-2002. Small right renal cyst is noted. The kidneys appear atrophic. There are marked degenerative changes at both shoulder joints, right greater than left. Multilevel degenerative changes are present in the thoracic spine. No lytic or blastic osseous lesions are seen. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. FINDINGS ARE MOST COMPATIBLE WITH DIFFUSE PULMONARY EDEMA WITH SMALL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS SUPERIMPOSED ON CHRONIC LUNG CHANGES. 3. MEDIASTINAL LYMPHADENOPATHY, LIKELY SECONDARY TO CHF. 4. PRELIMINARY REPORT PROVIDED BY RADIOLOGY RESIDENT DR. MCINTYRE BRYSON. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There are bilateral subsegmental pulmonary emboli. These are seen best on series 2, images 245 and 226 in the bilateral lower lobes. In addition, there is a linear filling defect within a right upper lobe subsegmental pulmonary artery seen best on series 2, image 324. This constellation is characteristic of multiple subsegmental pulmonary emboli. There are no large central or lobar pulmonary emboli. There are numerous pulmonary nodules seen throughout the lung parenchyma. There are approximately 9. The largest of these is 4 mm in size. This is seen within the right middle lobe. There are additional subpleural nodules which are also less than 4 mm in size. In a patient without risk factors for primary or secondary lung malignancy these are likely benign. There is no pleural or pericardial effusion. There is no axillary or intrathoracic lymphadenopathy. Evaluation of the upper abdomen is limited by scope and timing of contrast administration. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen is unremarkable. Osseous structures are unremarkable. IMPRESSION: 1. MULTILOBAR SUBSEGMENTAL PULMONARY EMBOLISM AS DESCRIBED. 2. THESE RESULTS WERE DISCUSSED WITH Boone MD AT 1010 ON 16-18-03. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

GENERAL RULES

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.
If the report lacks Findings, use the given text.
Ignore History, Technique, Dose, Imaging Devices, Administrative Text, and Boilerplate Parts of the report.
Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. input: FINDINGS: There are multiple new intraluminal filling defects within the segmental and subsegmental right upper lobe, right middle lobe, and left lower lobe pulmonary arteries. However, the overall clot burden has decreased when compared to study dated 1-2-2009. There is moderate centrilobular emphysematous change. There are small bilateral pleural effusions with associated compressive atelectasis and / or consolidation. There are nonspecific peripheral, wedge-shaped ground glass opacities within the bilateral upper lobes, possibly represent small pulmonary infarcts. There is mild increased interlobular septal thickening within the mid to lower lung zones bilaterally, suggesting pulmonary edema. There are two nonspecific biapical pulmonary nodules measuring approximately 5 mm (2/416, 2/417) stable in size when compared to study dated 1/2 / 2009. There is no pneumothorax. There is mild cardiomegaly with with reflux of contrast into the IVC and hepatic veins. Coronary artery calcifications are present. There is mild calcified atherosclerotic plaque within the thoracic aorta. The supraclavicular spaces are normal. There are multiple subcentimeter mediastinal and axillary lymph nodes which are more remarkable in number and than in size. Extensive retrocrural lymphadenopathy increased when compared to study dated JANUARY 2ND, 2009. The dominant right retrocrural lymph node measures 2.5 x 1.5 cm (2/96), previously measuring 1.5 x 0.8 cm. There are no pathologically enlarged supraclavicular lymph nodes by CT criteria There is extensive multilevel degenerative change of the visualized thoracic spine with old compression fracture deformities involving the T7, T8, T9, T10, L1, and L2 vertebral bodies. There is an old nonunited sternal body fracture with associate sclerosis is callus formation. There are multiple bilateral rib fracture deformities with associated callus formation. Redemonstration of multiple sclerotic and lytic lesions scattered throughout the visualized axial skeleton, unchanged when compared to study dated 1/2 / 2009. Please referred to dedicated abdominal CT dated 2009-16-10 for description of abdominal findings. IMPRESSION: 1. THERE ARE MULTIPLE NEW SEGMENTAL AND SUBSEGMENTAL RIGHT UPPER LOBE, RIGHT MIDDLE LOBE, AND LEFT LOWER LOBE PULMONARY EMBOLI WHEN COMPARED TO STUDY DATED 01-02-09. HOWEVER, THE OVERALL CLOT BURDEN HAS DECREASED WHEN COMPARED TO RECENT PRIOR IMAGING. 2. NONSPECIFIC PERIPHERAL, WEDGE-SHAPED GROUND-GLASS OPACITIES WITHIN THE BILATERAL UPPER LOBES, POSSIBLY REPRESENT SMALL PULMONARY INFARCTS, NEW WHEN COMPARED TO STUDY DATED 1/2 / 2009.3. SMALL BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS WITH ASSOCIATED COMPRESSIVE ATELECTASIS AND / OR CONSOLIDATION. 4. MODERATE CENTRILOBULAR EMPHYSEMATOUS CHANGE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

The main pulmonary artery is normal in size and has a maximum measurement of 22 mm. Evaluation of the mediastinal structures demonstrates a normal sized cardiac silhouette. The ascending aorta is also normal in size and appearance. There is a left-sided aortic arch with a normal cervical branching pattern. No significant mediastinal, hilar or axillary adenopathy is identified. There is no significant pericardial effusion. The central airways are patent. Evaluation of the lung parenchyma demonstrates a single one to 2-mm pulmonary nodule within the right middle lobe which is nonspecific however requires no further follow-up according to Fleischner society guidelines. The remainder of the lung parenchyma is unremarkable with no evidence of suspicious masses or areas of consolidation. There are no significant pleural effusions. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen demonstrates no significant abnormality. Evaluation of the bony structures demonstrates no significant abnormality. IMPRESSION: 1. input: FINDINGS: Contrast opacification of the pulmonary arterial circulation is excellent for assessment for PE. No filling defect to suggest pulmonary embolism. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Coronary artery calcifications are not seen. Heart is normal in appearance. No pericardial effusion. Aorta has a normal caliber and course. Main pulmonary artery is not enlarged. Innumerable pulmonary nodules are scattered throughout the lungs bilaterally. Largest measures approximately 0.9 cm in the superior segment of the right lower lobe. Moderate right and trace left apparently layering pleural effusions. Pleural nodularity is noted. In particular, on the right, there are a few soft tissue density pleural-based nodules which measure up to approximately 3.5 x 2.3 cm in axial dimensions. Dense consolidation is present in the right lower lobe. Low attenuating mucous secretions are noted within the airways within the consolidation. Mild right basilar atelectasis is present. A loculated pleural fluid collection is noted superiorly in the right hemithorax which measures approximately 2.7 x 1.2 cm in axial dimensions. No supraclavicular or axillary lymphadenopathy. Severe pectus excavatum is noted. Haller index measures 4.4. Bilateral breast implants are noted. A prominent right cardiophrenic lymph node measures approximately 0.7 cm in short axis dimension. Small hiatus hernia. Upper visualized abdomen is normal in appearance on this arterial phase study. No definite aggressive osseous lesions. Stranding is noted again thoracic spine. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. INNUMERABLE PULMONARY NODULES ARE CONSISTENT WITH METASTATIC DISEASE. 3. SOFT TISSUE DENSITY PLEURAL-BASED NODULES ON THE RIGHT LIKELY REFLECT METASTATIC DEPOSITS. IF THE PATIENT HAS HAD A RECENT THORACENTESIS, THE SOFT TISSUE DENSITY MAY ALSO REFLECT BLOOD, ALTHOUGH THIS IS LESS LIKELY. ASSOCIATED MODERATE RIGHT AND TRACE LEFT PLEURAL EFFUSIONS. 4. DENSE CONSOLIDATION IN THE RIGHT LOWER LOBE, LIKELY SECONDARY TO ATELECTASIS WITH POSSIBLE SUPERIMPOSED ASPIRATION / PNEUMONIA. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

No evidence of filling defect is seen within the pulmonary arterial system to suggest pulmonary embolus. Consistent with pulmonary hypertension, there is associated mild enlargement and trabeculation of the right ventricle, compression of the left ventricle, reflux of contrast into the IVC and hepatic veins. There is no evidence of anomalous pulmonary venous return or intracardiac shunt. The ascending aorta is normal in caliber. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy is present. The trachea and central airways are patent. Lungs are clear without focal consolidation, effusion, or edema. Visualized portions of the upper abdomen demonstrates a surgically absent gallbladder. Otherwise, visualized portions of the upper abdomen and are unremarkable. Visualized osseous structures demonstrate no suspicious osseous lesions. IMPRESSION: 1. ENLARGEMENT OF THE MAIN PULMONARY ARTERY MEASURING UP TO 40 MM, WITH TRABECULATION AND MILD ENLARGEMENT OF THE RIGHT VENTRICLE, CONSISTENT WITH HISTORY OF PULMONARY ARTERIAL HYPERTENSION. 2. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM, INTRACARDIAC SHUNT, OR ANOMALOUS PULMONARY VENOUS RETURN. 3. LUNGS ARE CLEAR. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: For abdomen and pelvis findings, please see separate dictation under accession # 102221701776. Multiple fractures: Minimally displaced slightly comminuted mid / distal left clavicular fracture with surrounding soft tissue swelling. Left rib 4 nondisplaced fracture along lateral extent. Left rib 5 nondisplaced fracture along lateral extent. Left rib 6 mildly displaced fracture along lateral extent. Left rib 7 moderately displaced fracture along posterior / lateral aspect. Left rib 8 moderately displaced comminuted fracture along posterior / lateral aspect. No sternal or thoracic spine fracture. Incidental note of bifid right rib 5. Adjacent to the rib fractures, there is a small amount of intrathoracic air, but no over pneumothorax, this air may be extra pleural. Additional small amount of soft tissue emphysema is present along the rib fracture sites. Tiny amount of lung contusion adjacent to rib fractures. Small left hemothorax effusion. Bibasilar dependent atelectasis. Airways are patent and of normal course and caliber. No evidence of traumatic aortic injury. Visualized portions of the aorta are normal in course and caliber. The heart size is normal without a pericardial effusion. Main pulmonary artery is normal in course and caliber. Limited evaluation for pulmonary emboli demonstrates no filling defects. No mediastinal, hilar or axillary lymphadenopathy. IMPRESSION: 1. LEFT RIB 4--8 FRACTURES AND LEFT CLAVICULAR FRACTURE AS DESCRIBED. SMALL LEFT HEMOTHORAX AND POSSIBLE SMALL LEFT LUNG CONTUSION ADJACENT TO THE RIB FRACTURES. SMALL AMOUNT OF AIR ALONG THE FRACTURE SITES, BUT NO OVERT PNEUMOTHORAX. 2. NO EVIDENCE OF TRAUMATIC AORTIC INJURY. 3. FOR ABDOMEN AND PELVIS FINDINGS, PLEASE SEE SEPARATE DICTATION UNDER ACCESSION 102221701776.4. PRIMARY RESULTS PROVIDED TO Connor D Odonnell M. D. (TRAUMA) BY ON CALL RADIOLOGY RESIDENT AT 2245 ON MARCH 2014. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The heart size is normal without a pericardial effusion. The aorta is normal in course and caliber. There is a two vessel aortic arch with the brachiocephalic and left common carotid artery sharing a common origin. The pulmonary arterial tree is well opacified, without filling defects to suggest a large pulmonary embolism, although the study is markedly limited by motion artifact. The main pulmonary artery is normal in course but is enlarged and measures 3.4 cm. There is mild dependent atelectasis, the lungs are otherwise clear. No pleural effusions. The airways are patent and of normal course and caliber. No mediastinal, hilar or axillary lymphadenopathy. There are multiple small mediastinal lymph nodes. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen is unremarkable. No suspicious bony lesions identified. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM, ALTHOUGH THE STUDY IS LIMITED DUE TO MOTION ARTIFACT. 2. ENLARGED PULMONARY ARTERY WHICH MAY BE SEEN WITH PULMONARY HYPERTENSION. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The examination is limited due to respiratory motion artifact. Evaluation of the segmental and subsegmental pulmonary arteries is limited particularly in the lung bases. No central pulmonary embolism is identified. The heart is mildly enlarged. Extensive coronary artery calcification is noted. There is no pericardial effusion. No pathologic lymph node enlargement identified within the chest. Although limited in evaluation due to respiratory motion, there appears to be subtle airspace opacity within the right middle lobe and possibly mild opacity in the left lower lobe. There are no pleural effusions. No pneumothorax. There is a new 7-mm right lower lobe nodule (5/98). Degenerative changes are seen throughout the thoracic spine with marked kyphosis. No acute bony abnormality is identified. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE FOR PULMONARY EMBOLISM, HOWEVER EVALUATION OF THE SMALL SEGMENTAL AND SUBSEGMENTAL BRANCHES IS MARKEDLY LIMITED BY RESPIRATORY MOTION ARTIFACT. 2. MILD AIRSPACE OPACITY WITHIN THE RIGHT MIDDLE LOBE AND TO A LESSER EXTENT LEFT LOWER LOBE. CONSIDER PNEUMONIA AND / OR ATELECTASIS. 3. NEW 7-MM NODULE IN THE RIGHT LOWER LOBE. RECOMMEND COMPARISON WITH ANY AVAILABLE OUTSIDE IMAGING. IF NONE AVAILABLE, RECOMMEND FOLLOW UP PER FLEISCHNER SOCIETY CRITERIA FOR INCIDENTAL PULMONARY NODULES BETWEEN 7 AND 8 MM. LOW RISK PATIENTS: CT FOLLOW UP AT 6-12 MONTHS AND 18-24 MONTHS. HIGH RISK PATIENTS: CT FOLLOW UP AT 3-6 MONTHS, 9-12 MONTHS, AND 24 MONTHS. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

There is some scattered ground glass opacity in the lung bases, could be secondary to hypoaeration and / or motion. Thyroid: Multiple small low-density nodules noted. Small hiatal hernia. The main pulmonary artery is normal in course and caliber Two small pulmonary nodules are noted in the right upper lobe measuring 3 to 4 mm in the lateral aspect (series 2 image 376) and 2 mm in the medial aspect (series 2 image 360). Otherwise the lungs appear clear without focal consolidation. No pleural effusions. The airways are patent and of normal course and caliber. No mediastinal, hilar or axillary lymphadenopathy. Post-surgical changes related to bilateral mastectomy and reconstruction. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen is unremarkable. No suspicious bony lesions identified. Addendum Begins Addendum: The pulmonary nodules described on the CT Pulmonary Embolism study dated 12-10 were compared with the prior outside CT dated 09, JULY 15, and appear stable, compatible with benign nodules. Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. VERY SMALL PULMONARY NODULES IN THE RIGHT UPPER LOBE, AS DESCRIBED ABOVE; COMPARISON WITH AVAILABLE PRIOR STUDIES WOULD BE HELPFUL FOR FURTHER EVALUATION. 3. FINDINGS DISCUSSED WITH THE ORDERING PHYSICIAN STERLING J MD AT 11:55 ON 10/10/12. SUMMARY: 1-NO SIGNIFICANT ABNORMALITY I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

instruction:
Your task is extracting medical entities from a single radiology report and returning them as a strictly defined relation graph in JSON format.

The output must be a SINGLE JSON ARRAY of relation objects following this structure:

```json
[
  {
    "anchor": "str (field:status)",
    "related": ["str (field:status)"]
  }
]
```

Where:

- "field" is one of: "observation", "location", "degree", "trend", "measurement"
- "status" is: 
  - "present", "absent", or "uncertain" for "observation", "location", "degree", "trend" fields.
  - "current" or "old" for "measurement" field.

Do NOT output anything other than JSON array.

GENERAL RULES

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.
If the report lacks Findings, use the given text.
Ignore History, Technique, Dose, Imaging Devices, Administrative Text, and Boilerplate Parts of the report.
Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: FINDINGS: Thyroid: Imaged portions of the thyroid gland are unremarkable. Thoracic aorta: The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber and demonstrates standard 3-vessel arch anatomy. A left-sided infusion port is noted. Heart: The heart is normal in size and configuration, without pericardial effusion. Pulmonary arteries: There is no evidence of pulmonary embolus in the main, lobar, or segmental pulmonary arteries. Bronchial arteries: The bronchial arteries are enlarged, right greater than left. The right bronchial artery is tortuous and arises from the descending thoracic aorta at the level of T6. The bronchial artery arises at the level of T5. Lungs / Pleura: There is diffuse bronchiectasis with an upper lobe predominance, similar in appearance to the 9-8-2021 examination and consistent with the patient's history of cystic fibrosis. Diffuse bronchial wall thickening with multiple regions of mucoid impaction are again seen. There are numerous foci of centrilobular nodularity, with more focal consolidative changes in the right middle lobe that is new when compared to 9/8 / 2021. There is significant ground glass attenuation to suggest acute hemorrhage. No cavitary lesions are identified. Mediastinum:. There are enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes. The largest is in the right paratracheal region and measures 1.2-cm in short axis. Hila: There is bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with nodes measuring up to 1.2-cm in short axis. Axillae: Clear. Upper abdomen: Partially imaged upper abdominal viscera is grossly unremarkable. Skeletal structures: Unremarkable. IMPRESSION: 1. CHRONIC CHANGES CONSISTENT WITH THE PROVIDED HISTORY OF CYSTIC FIBROSIS. 2. ENLARGED BRONCHIAL ARTERIES AS DESCRIBED ABOVE. THERE IS NO ACTIVE CONTRAST EXTRAVASATION. 3. THERE IS NO EVIDENCE OF ACUTE PARENCHYMAL HEMORRHAGE OR MYCETOMA AS CLINICALLY QUESTIONED. 4. CONSOLIDATIVE CHANGES IN THE RIGHT MIDDLE LOBE HAVE INCREASED COMPARED TO 9/8 / 2021. THIS COULD REFLECT EXACERBATION OF AN INFECTIOUS OR INFLAMMATORY PROCESS. RESOLVING HEMATOMA IS CONSIDERED LESS LIKELY. 5. MEDIASTINAL AND HILAR LYMPH ADENOPATHY IS LIKELY REACTIVE. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: PULMONARY ARTERIES: There is adequate opacification of the pulmonary arteries up to the level of the subsegmental branches. The main pulmonary artery is normal in caliber. No arterial filling defects or abnormal tapering in the visualized portions of pulmonary arteries.. No evidence of pulmonary arterio-venous malformation. LUNGS: There is biapical pleuro-parenchymal cystic change which is somewhat atypical for prior granulomatous disease. There is a likely accessory fissure within the right lower lobe (series 601, image 48; series 3, image 147). No evidence of pleural effusion or pneumothorax. On ser 3, imag 250 there is a 2 by 4 mm nodule noted of unclear cinical significance. MEDIASTINUM: The heart is normal in size. No coronary calcifications are present. The aorta is normal in caliber and contour. No evidence of aneurysm or dissection. No significant atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta is present. No significant lymphadenopathy in the mediastinum or hilum. BONES: The bony structures of the thorax appear normal. MISC: No significant lymphadenopathy is present in the visualized portions of the neck, thoracic inlet, and bilateral axilla. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen is grossly normal. Addendum Begins 3D reformations consisting of curved and multiplanar reformations, maximum intensity projections, and volume rendered images were performed on an independent workstation and corroborate the findings. Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLUS. 2. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY ARTERIO-VENOUS MALFORMATION, AS CLINICALLY QUERIED. 3. BIAPICAL PLEURO-PARENCHYMAL CYSTIC CHANGE IS SOMEWHAT ATYPICAL IN APPEARANCE FOR PRIOR GRANULOMATOUS DISEASE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The pulmonary arteries are well adequately opacified with contrast. However, there is extensive motion artifact, particularly at the bases. The motion renders the study inadequate to evaluate for segmental and subsegmental pulmonary embolus. However, there is no evidence of pulmonary embolus to the lobar pulmonary artery level. The main pulmonary artery is dilated measuring over 4 cm in diameter, which is a nonspecific finding but can be seen in pulmonary hypertension. Additionally, there is mild right ventricular dilatation as well as right ventricular wall thickening, concerning for cor pulmonale. There are extensive centrilobular emphysematous changes in the lungs. There is no pulmonary consolidation. There is no mediastinal, hilar, or axillary lymphadenopathy. Aorta is of normal caliber. Evaluation of the upper abdomen reveals a simple cyst within the left kidney as well as gallstones. IMPRESSION: 1. EXAM LIMITED BY PRESENCE OF RESPIRATORY MOTION, THERE IS NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLUS UP TO THE LOBAR PULMONARY ARTERIES. HOWEVER, EVALUATION OF THE SEGMENTAL AND SUBSEGMENTAL PULMONARY ARTERIES IS NOT POSSIBLE DUE TO MARKED MOTION ARTIFACT. 2. MILD RIGHT VENTRICULAR DILATATION AS WELL AS RIGHT VENTRICULAR WALL THICKENING AND PULMONARY ARTERIAL DILATATION, CONCERNING FOR COR PULMONALE. 3. EXTENSIVE CENTRILOBULAR EMPHYSEMATOUS CHANGES IN THE LUNGS. 4. GALLSTONES. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION Major findings were made available by the on-call resident on 6/01 at 2009. I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Evaluation of the pulmonary arteries demonstrates no filling defects to suggest pulmonary emboli. There has been significant interval progression of metastatic disease when compared with the prior study. There has been interval increase in size of numerous (dozens) for pulmonary nodules and masses scattered throughout both lungs, the largest of which measures 4 cm in the left upper lobe, previously measuring 2.2-cm. There is stable appearance of a large multinodular thyroid goiter with focal hypodense nodules. The great vessels are unremarkable and the central airways are patent. There is a Mediport in the right danger chest wall, with the tip near the cavoatrial junction. No pathologically enlarged axillary, mediastinal, or hilar lymph nodes are identified. The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. Evaluation of the bones demonstrates numerous osteolytic lesions with associated soft tissue masses, progressed since the prior study. Involved areas include both scapula and humeral heads, clavicles numerous bilateral ribs, and multiple vertebral bodies. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen demonstrates interval increase in size of a soft tissue metastatic nodule in the right anterior abdominal wall, now measuring 2.1 cm. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. EXTENSIVE INTERVAL PROGRESSION OF METASTATIC DISEASE IN THE CHEST, SOFT TISSUES, AND OSSEOUS STRUCTURES WHEN COMPARED WITH THE PRIOR STUDY DATED 2015 April 30. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: No evidence of pulmonary thromboembolism. No evidence of elevated right-sided heart pressures; main pulmonary artery normal in caliber. Normal heart size. Right cardiophrenic lymphadenopathy, measuring up to 1.2 cm in short axis (image 13, series 3). No suspicious osseous lesions. Addendum Begins Multiplanar reformations and maximum intensity projections were performed on an independent 3D workstation and corroborate the findings. Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. No evidence of pulmonary thromboembolism. 2. Right axillary and right cardiophrenic lymphadenopathy, concerning for metastatic disease. 3. Mild right pleural effusion. 4. input: FINDINGS: There are interval changes of median sternotomy, with small amount of subcutaneous fluid / edema, and subcutaneous gas, superficial to a midline sternotomy with wires (which are intact). There's been interval graft replacement of the ascending aorta, extending from the sinotubular junction to the proximal aortic arch. The graft appears normal, without complication. No pseudoaneurysm or leak is evident. There is a moderate amount of postoperative fluid surrounding the aorta, without rim enhancement or evidence of blood products. Small amount of postoperative gas remains present in the mediastinum and along the anterior aspects of the pleural spaces. The aortic arch again appears normal, without evidence of acute abnormality. There is 4-vessel variant arch anatomy, with an apparent origin of the right subclavian artery as the first branch. The descending thoracic aorta appears normal. The heart size is again seen to be normal. There is a small amount of loculated pericardial fluid, related to recent surgery. The main pulmonary artery is normal in course and caliber. While not a dedicated pulmonary embolism study, no filling defects are seen in the main or lobar pulmonary arteries to suggest pulmonary embolism. The lungs appear clear. Moderate-sized layering pleural effusions are present, with passive atelectasis of the dependent basilar segments of the bilateral lower lobes. The airways are patent and of normal course and caliber. No mediastinal, hilar or axillary lymphadenopathy. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen is unremarkable. Other than the acute sternotomy, and mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine, the osseous structures are unremarkable. Preliminary report, which does not differ significantly from the final report, was provided by the resident on call at 0859 hours on May 13th, 2019. Based on a 32 cm body phantom, the estimated radiation dose (CTDIvol mGy) for each series in this exam are: 10.4 (noncontrast chest), 23 (CT angiogram chest) mGy. The estimated cumulative dose (DLP mGy-cm) is: 1224 mGy-cm. NOTE: The radiation dose indicators for CT--the'volume CT Dose Index'(CTDIvol) given in milli-Gray (mGy), and the Dose Length Product (DLP) given in mGy-centimeters (mGy. cm)--are generated from the CT scanner to estimate radiation exposure based on technical study parameters and a reference phantom. The CTDI and DLP may therefore substantially over-or underestimate an individual's absorbed dose based on patient size and other factors. Addendum Begins *** Preliminary measurements were made during primary interpretation. The following measurements were provided for treatment planning in the 3D Laboratory on an independent workstation: **** **** Start of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** AccNO: 64829219 Diameters in mm Major Minor Aorta at arch 46.50 32.80 Aortic annulus 30.40 22.90 Aorta at sinuses of valsalva 43.80 37.60 Aorta at sinotubular junction 31.40 29.40 Ascending aorta 30.70 29.70 **** End of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. STATUS POST ACUTE ASCENDING AORTA INTERPOSITION GRAFT REPAIR, EXTENDING FROM THE SINOTUBULAR JUNCTION TO THE PROXIMAL AORTIC ARCH WITHOUT EVIDENCE OF COMPLICATION. EXPECTED POSTOPERATIVE FLUID AND GAS WITHIN THE MEDIASTINUM AND ANTERIOR PLEURAL SPACES. 2. MODERATE BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS, WITH ASSOCIATED PASSIVE ATELECTASIS OF THE DEPENDENT BASILAR LOWER LUNGS. SUMMARY: 1-NO SIGNIFICANT ABNORMALITY I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There are no intraluminal filling defects within the pulmonary arteries to suggest pulmonary emboli. The main pulmonary artery is enlarged measuring 3.6-cm in maximal transverse dimension, a finding that may be seen in pulmonary hypertension in the appropriate clinical setting. Moderate size bilateral pleural effusions with associated compressive atelectasis and / or consolidation, mildly increased in size when compared to study dated 10/07/2006. Nonspecific pulmonary nodules measuring 4-6 mm within the lungs bilaterally (3/190, 3/176, 3/162, 3/156, 3/93, 3/90, and 3/88) are stable in size and distribution when compared to study dated 10-7-2006. There is no pneumothorax. There is no cardiomegaly nor pericardial effusion. Coronary artery calcifications are present. There is mild calcified atherosclerotic plaque within the thoracic aorta. The supraclavicular spaces are normal. Mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy is stable in size when compared to study dated 10-7-2006. Dominant right paratracheal lymph node measures 1.8 x 1.0 cm (3/170), previously measuring 1.6 x 1.1 cm. Dominant precarinal lymph node measures 2.1 x 0.9 cm (3/158), previously measuring 2.0 x 1.0 cm. Dominant AP window lymph node measures 1.7 x 1.6 cm (3/163), previously measuring 2.0 x 1.3 cm. Dominant right hilar lymph node measures 2.5 x 1.0 cm (3/145). There are no pathologically enlarged supraclavicular nor axillary lymph nodes by CT criteria. Bilateral subcentimeter axillary lymph nodes unremarkable and number than in size. There are numerous subtle sclerotic lesions scattered throughout the visualized portions of the thoracolumbar spine, multiple right-sided ribs, and sternum worrisome for lymphomatous involvement. There is mild multilevel degenerative change of the visualized thoracolumbar spine. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLUS. 2. MODERATE SIZE BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS WITH ASSOCIATED COMPRESSIVE ATELECTASIS AND / OR CONSOLIDATION, MILDLY INCREASED IN SIZE WHEN COMPARED TO STUDY DATED 10/7 / 2006.3. NONSPECIFIC PULMONARY NODULES MEASURING 4-6 MM WITHIN THE LUNGS BILATERALLY ARE STABLE IN SIZE AND DISTRIBUTION WHEN COMPARED TO STUDY DATED 10/7 / 2006. GIVEN CLINICAL HISTORY OF RECENT BONE MARROW TRANSPLANT, FINDINGS REMAIN WORRISOME FOR AN UNDERLYING INFECTIOUS PROCESS, INCLUDING FUNGAL ETIOLOGIES. 4. MEDIASTINAL LYMPHADENOPATHY IS STABLE WHEN COMPARED TO STUDY DATED 2006/10/07.5. SUBTLE SCLEROTIC LESIONS SCATTERED THROUGHOUT THE AXIAL SKELETON ARE WORRISOME FOR LYMPHOMATOUS INVOLVEMENT. RECOMMEND FURTHER CHARACTERIZATION WITH A DEDICATED NUCLEAR MEDICINE BONE SCAN. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: A tiny hypodense nodule seen in the right lobe of the thyroid. The heart size is normal. There is a tiny pericardial effusion. The aorta and its major branches are normal in course and caliber. The main pulmonary artery is upper limits normal for size measuring 30 mm in diameter and stable from the 05-09-22 study. This is a nonspecific finding, but can be seen in the setting of pulmonary arterial hypertension. There is good opacification of the pulmonary arterial tree, with no filling defects to suggest pulmonary embolism. The innumerable pulmonary nodules in a perilymphatic distribution are all slightly larger and more conspicuous than in the prior study, with a more confluent and consolidative appearance at the bases. Airway thickening is also increased predominantly at the lung bases. The central airways appear patent. No pleural effusion or pneumothorax is appreciated. Postbiopsy changes are seen within the right lower lobe posteriorly. There is extensive supraclavicular, axillary, mediastinal, hilar and upper abdominal lymphadenopathy, not significantly changed from the prior study, with representative lymph nodes as follows: 13-mm AP window node, 11-mm precarinal node, 11-mm left hilar node, two 18-mm right axillary node, and a 12-mm celiac node. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen otherwise appears unremarkable. The bones are diffusely heterogeneous and mottled in appearance, with patchy areas of sclerosis. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. INCREASED SIZE AND CONSPICUITY OF INNUMERABLE PULMONARY NODULES, MANY OF WHICH ARE IN A PERILYMPHATIC DISTRIBUTION, WITH A MORE CONSOLIDATIVE APPEARANCE AT THE LUNG BASES. FINDINGS ARE WORRISOME FOR RAPID LYMPHOMATOUS PROGRESSION, THOUGH SUPERIMPOSED INFECTION IS DIFFICULT TO EXCLUDE. 3. EXTENSIVE LYMPHADENOPATHY, AND PATCHY BONY ABNORMALITIES, IN KEEPING WITH LYMPHOMATOUS INVOLVEMENT. 4. PRELIMINARY FINDINGS WERE REPORTED ELECTRONICALLY ON 05, October 13th 6:42 P. M. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: PULMONARY ANGIOGRAM: There is no pulmonary embolism. The pulmonary artery and its segmental and subsegmental branches enhance normally with contrast. The pulmonary artery is enlarged measuring up to 3.5 cm in the transverse dimension. There is cardiomegaly. There is no pericardial effusion. There is a 3 vessel branching left aortic arch that is in normal in caliber throughout its visualized course. There is atherosclerotic disease with calcification of the aorta. There is patchy ground glass attenuation in the lingula that may be infectious / inflammatory in etiology. There is dependent bilateral atelectasis. There are no pleural effusions. There is no pneumothorax. There is no focal area of consolidation or nodularity. There is no mediastinal or hilar adenopathy. The thyroid gland is unremarkable. ABDOMEN / PELVIS: There is a surgical drain coursing along the subcutaneous tissues of the anterior abdominal wall; the tip of the drain lies in anterior right abdominal wall. There is no free fluid or abscess collection in this region. There are anchors from the surgical mesh along the anterior abdominal wall with punctate foci of gas and fluid in the intra-abdominal wall fascia in keeping with the patient's recent surgery. No well-defined fluid collection is identified. There are postsurgical changes of the gastrointestinal tract in keeping with a prior sigmoid colectomy with re-anastomosis. There are fluid-filled loops of large and small bowel. The bowel wall enhances normally. There is no pneumatosis or free intraperitoneal air. There is no free intraperitoneal fluid. There is hepatic steatosis. The gallbladder has been surgically removed. There is no intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The spleen, adrenal glands, and pancreas are unremarkable. There are bilateral extrarenal pelvises. There is a 4-mm calculus in the interpolar region of the left kidney that may be calyceal or vascular in etiology (Se 3 Im 141). There is symmetrical excretion of contrast on the delayed images. There is no hydronephrosis. There is a punctate 7-mm cortical hypoattenuating lesion in the inferior pole the left kidney that is too small to characterize. There is an exophytic 1.4-cm cyst off the superior pole of the right kidney. There is moderate atherosclerotic disease of the aorta with no aneurysmal dilatation. There is streak artifact in the pelvis from the right total hip replacement limiting evaluation of this area. A Foley catheter is visualized within the urinary bladder. There are multilevel degenerative changes of the spine with fusion of the L2-L3 vertebral bodies. Addendum Begins *** Preliminary measurements were made during primary interpretation. The following protocol measurements were obtained in the 3D Laboratory on an independent workstation: **** **** Start of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** AccNO: HTAWLYGERRCTN Diameters in mm Major Minor Ascending aorta 37.80 37.20 **** End of 3D Lab Measurements Report **** Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. NO PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. PATCHY GROUND GLASS ATTENUATION IN THE LINGULA AND MAY BE IN KEEPING WITH AN INFECTIOUS / INFLAMMATORY ETIOLOGY. 3. ENLARGED PULMONARY ARTERY MAY BE SEEN IN THE SETTING OF PULMONARY HYPERTENSION. CARDIOMEGALY. 4. POSTSURGICAL CHANGES ALONG THE ANTERIOR ABDOMINAL WALL WITH A SURGICAL DRAIN IN PLACE ADJACENT TO GAS AND FLUID IN THE ABDOMINAL FASCIA. NO WELL-DEFINED FLUID COLLECTION IS IDENTIFIED. 5. FLUID-FILLED LOOPS OF LARGE AND SMALL BOWEL MAY BE IN KEEPING WITH POSTOPERATIVE ILEUS. 6. HEPATIC STEATOSIS. VERBAL REPORT PROVIDED BY THE ON-CALL RESIDENT ON 01/13/2011 AT 0614. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The main pulmonary artery measures 3.1 cm in diameter, which is nonspecific but may be seen in the setting of pulmonary arterial hypertension. There appear to be mild aortic annular calcifications. Mild atherosclerotic calcifications of the aorta are demonstrated. The pericardium is within normal limits. Paratracheal nodes measure up to 1.1 cm. There is a 1.3 cm subcarinal node. Paraaortic nodes measure up to 1.1 cm. Evaluation for hilar nodes is limited due to non-contiguous imaging and lack of intravenous contrast. The central airways are patent. No pleural effusion or pneumothorax is identified. There are scattered areas of bronchiectasis and bronchial distortion, worst in both upper lobes. Scattered reticular changes are noted. In addition, there may be mild honeycombing, for example, in the right lower lobe (series 2 image 49). A few nonspecific nodular opacities are identified, for example, in the superior segment of the left lower lobe (series 2 image 28) and in the right lower lobe (series 2 image 37). Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen is grossly unremarkable. The osseous structures demonstrate degenerative changes. IMPRESSION: 1. SCATTERED AREAS OF BRONCHIECTASIS AND BRONCHIAL DISTORTION, WORST IN THE UPPER LOBES. THERE ARE ALSO SCATTERED RETICULAR CHANGES AND SOME HONEYCOMBING. THESE FINDINGS ARE COMPATIBLE WITH FIBROTIC PULMONARY SARCOIDOSIS. 2. DEMONSTRATION OF SCATTERED FOCAL NODULAR OPACITIES WHICH ARE NONSPECIFIC. CORRELATION WITH ANY AVAILABLE PRIOR EXAMS IS SUGGESTED. OTHERWISE, ATTENTION ON FOLLOWUP IS RECOMMENDED. 3. THE MAIN PULMONARY ARTERY MEASURES 3.1 CM IN DIAMETER WHICH IS NONSPECIFIC BUT MAY BE SEEN IN THE SETTING OF PULMONARY ARTERIAL HYPERTENSION. 4. MEDIASTINAL LYMPHADENOPATHY COMPATIBLE WITH SARCOID. EVALUATION FOR HILAR NODES IS LIMITD DUE TO NON-CONTIGUOUS IMAGING AND LACK OF INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The heart size is normal without a pericardial effusion. Significant calcification is seen in the bilateral coronary arteries. The aorta is normal in course and caliber. No definite evidence of aortic dissection or aneurysm; however, the study is limited by cardiac motion artifact. Calcified plaque is seen at the origin of the great vessels, celiac artery, and bilateral renal arteries without evidence of obstruction. The main pulmonary artery is normal in course and caliber. While not a dedicated pulmonary embolism study, no filling defects are seen in the main or lobar pulmonary arteries to suggest pulmonary embolism. The lungs demonstrate bandlike opacities within the left lower lobe. The right lung is clear. No significant pleural effusions. The airways are patent and of normal course and caliber. Multiple lymph nodes are identified in the right hilar, subcarinal, and bilateral axilla regions. A 15-mm mass is seen in the region of the right breast / axilla. This lesion is partially visualized on the previous study and appears stable in size. Recommend correlation with mammography findings. Evaluation of the abdomen demonstrates multiple hypodense liver lesions compatible with cysts. The common bile duct and pancreatic duct appear prominent with no evidence of stones or other secondary signs of obstruction or infection. The spleen, bilateral adrenal glands, and kidneys are unremarkable. No evidence of bowel ischemia, obstruction, or inflammation. The bones demonstrate minimal degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE AORTIC DISSECTION OR ANEURYSM. 2. INTERVAL APPEARANCE OF LEFT LOWER LOBE BANDLIKE OPACITY, ATELECTASIS VERSUS FOCAL CONSOLIDATION. 3. SIGNIFICANT ATHEROSCLEROSIS OF THE CORONARY VESSELS AND ORIGINS OF THE GREAT VESSELS, CELIAC ARTERY, AND BILATERAL RENAL ARTERIES. 4. PROMINENT COMMON BILE DUCT AND PANCREATIC DUCT WITHOUT OTHER RADIOGRAPHIC FINDINGS TO SUGGEST OBSTRUCTION OR INFECTION. FINDING LIKELY REPRESENTS A PHYSIOLOGIC VARIANT. 5. MULTIPLE HYPODENSE LESIONS WITHIN THE LIVER ARE COMPATIBLE WITH CYSTS, STABLE SINCE 1/15/02. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

Metallic artifacts are identified along the path of the ligamentum arteriosus, compatible with prior occlusion of a patent ductus arteriosus. The remainder of the descending thoracic aorta is unremarkable. The pulmonary artery is grossly unremarkable. Incidental note is made of asymmetric hemithoraces with the right hemithorax smaller than the left. There are small pleural based nodules identified within the thorax that are of uncertain clinical significance. Incidental note is made of a persistent left SVC, a congenital variant. This drains into the coronary sinus. The visualized upper abdominal organs are unremarkable. There are no acute osseous abnormalities. IMPRESSION: 1. STATUS-POST REPAIR OF A COARCTATION OF THE AORTA. ABERRANT RIGHT SUBCLAVIAN ARTERY AS DESCRIBED, AS WELL AS PERSISTENT LEFT SVC AS DESCRIBED.
