input: FINDINGS: VASCULATURE: The right brachiocephalic, subclavian, axillary, brachial, interosseous, and radial / ulnar arteries are patent. These vessels are normal in caliber and morphology without evidence of aneurysmal dilatation nor focal dissection. There are no intraluminal filling defects to suggest embolic disease. The right deep palmer and superficial palmar arches are patent. The1st / 2nd-2nd / 3rd intermetacarpal and dorsal 5th digital arteries, arising from the superficial palmar arch, are moderately attenuated. The aortic arch is unremarkable without evidence of aneurysmal dilatation nor dissection. The great vessels are normal. The left vertebral artery arises from the aortic arch. CHEST: The lung apices are unremarkable without evidence of focal consolidations nor pleural effusions. There is no pneumothorax. There is increased soft tissue attenuation within the anterior mediastinum, likely representing residual thymic tissue. There are no pathologically enlarged right axillary, supraclavicular, nor superior mediastinal lymph nodes by CT criteria. The supraclavicular spaces are clear. The visualized osseous structures and soft tissues are normal. No suspicious lytic / blastic osseous lesions are identified. IMPRESSION: 1. ANATOMY: THE RIGHT DEEP PALMAR ARCH OFF THE RADIAL ARTERY IS INTACT WITH DOMINANT BLOOD SUPPLY TO THE DEEP METACARPALS SUPPLYING DIGITS I, II, AND RADIAL ASPECT OF DIG. III. THE SUPERFICIAL PALMAR ARCH OFF THE ULNAR ARTERY IS HYPOPLASTIC / INCOMPLETE, AND SUPPLIES THE DEEP METACARPALS TO DIGI. V (ULNAR) AND IV, III, WITH A DIMINUTIVE VESSEL CONNECTING TO THE RADIAL ARTERY TERRITORY. 2. THE METACARPALS TO 1ST / 2ND-2ND / 3RD INTERMETACARPAL AS WELL AS THE 5TH METACARPAL ARTERY, ARISING FROM THE SUPERFICIAL PALMAR ARCH, ARE MODERATELY ATTENUATED. FINDINGS MAY REPRESENT NORMAL ARTERIAL ANATOMY WITH VASOSPASM NOT ENTIRELY EXCLUDED. 3. THERE IS NO RADIOGRAPHIC EVIDENCE TO SUGGEST EMBOLIC DISEASE INVOLVING THE RIGHT UPPER EXTREMITY ARTERIAL CIRCULATION. 4. THERE IS NO RADIOGRAPHIC EVIDENCE OF FOCAL DISSECTION NOR ANEURYSMAL DILATATION INVOLVING THE RIGHT UPPER EXTREMITY ARTERIAL CIRCULATION. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is biprosthetic composite valve replacement of the aortic valve and ascending aorta. There is anastomosis of the graft with the native aorta at the junction of the distal ascending aorta and proximal transverse aorta. There is mediastinal gas and hematoma. There is no extraluminal contrast. The right and left coronary arteries arise from their respective cusps. The patient demonstrates a right dominant coronary branching anatomy. The study was not targeted for the evaluation of the coronaries. These appear patent proximally. Biventricular function appears unremarkable on cine images. The remainder of the native thoracic aorta appears unremarkable without evidence of aneurysm or dissection. The arch demonstrates a bovine branching pattern with the common origin at the right brachiocephalic and left common carotid arteries. There are right greater than left pleural effusions. There is compressive atelectasis within the right lower lobe. There is likely compressive atelectasis within the left lower lobe although there is some mild ground-glass more superiorly, which is nonspecific. There is no intrathoracic or axillary lymphadenopathy. Evaluation of the upper abdomen is limited by scope and timing of contrast. The limited evaluation of the upper abdomen demonstrates elevation of the left hemidiaphragm. input: FINDINGS: Of note, the study is limited due to respiratory motion, and contrast bolus timing, with suboptimal opacification of the pulmonary arterial system. The thyroid gland is diminutive in nature. Scattered small mediastinal and hilar nodes are seen. An 11-mm right hilar node is additionally present. The cardiac silhouette is within normal size limits. No pericardial effusion is present. The thoracic aorta is normal in course and caliber. The esophagus and gastroesophageal junction are grossly unremarkable. The main pulmonary artery is within normal size limits. No filling defects to suggest pulmonary emboli. No pleural effusions are present. The trachea and central bronchi are patent. Evaluation of the lung parenchyma demonstrates demonstrates a mild amount left greater than right basilar dependent atelectasis. Several pulmonary nodules are seen, including a 5-mm nodule within the right lower lobe, series 2 image 147, a subpleural 5-mm nodule also within the right lower lobe, image 147 as well as a 5-mm nodule within the left lower lobe, image 202. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen demonstrates no significant abnormality. Visualized osseous structures are grossly unremarkable. IMPRESSION: 1. THIS IS A LIMITED PE STUDY DUE TO RESPIRATORY MOTION AND POOR CONTRAST BOLUS TIMING HOWEVER NO CENTRAL OR SEGMENTAL PULMONARY EMBOLISM IS IDENTIFIED. THIS STUDY CANNOT ADEQUATELY EVALUATE FOR SMALL PERIPHERAL SUBSEGMENTAL PULMONARY EMBOLI. 2. A SINGLE 1 CM RIGHT HILAR NODE IS NONSPECIFIC. CORRELATION WITH PRIOR IMAGING MAY BE OF ASSISTANCE. 3. SEVERAL SMALL PULMONARY NODULES AS DESCRIBED: ACCORDING TO FLEISCHNER SOCIETY RECOMMENDATIONS, A PATIENT AT LOW RISK FOR MALIGNANCY WITH A 4 TO 6-MM PULMONARY NODULE NEEDS FOLLOW UP CT AT 12 MONTHS. IF THE PATIENT IS AT HIGH RISK FOR MALIGNANCY, FOLLOW-UP CT IN 6 TO 12 MONTHS IS RECOMMENDED. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: Visualized portions of the thyroid gland are unremarkable. No supraclavicular, axillary, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Lack of intravenous contrast limits evaluation of the hila. Ascending aortic caliber is normal. The main pulmonary artery measures 35 mm in diameter, which is dilated and suggestive of pulmonary arterial hypertension. Heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. There is coronary and thoracic aortic arteriosclerosis. Mitral and aortic annular calcification is incidentally noted. Evaluation of the lung parenchyma demonstrates extensive peribronchial thickening, irregular parenchymal lines, peripheral intralobular and sublobular reticulation, accompanied by areas of occasional architectural distortion and microcystic honeycombing, particularly noted within the mid and upper lung zones. There is mild traction bronchiectasis and bronchiolectasis, not appreciably out of proportion relative to surrounding parenchymal disease. No significant ground-glass or consolidative lung disease. Lung parenchyma demonstrates an overall mosaic attenuation pattern on the provided inspiratory phase images; however, expiratory phase imaging was not performed for assessment of air-trapping degree or extent. Pulmonary architectural lines are identified within the more radiolucent lobules, although intralobular broncho-arteriolar structures do appear relatively attenuated. Although not entirely confirmatory without expiratory phase imaging, pattern suggests hyperinflated radiolucent lobules with admixture of normal lung parenchyma, rather than true ground-glass lung disease. No discrete suspicious pulmonary nodules. No pleural effusions. Trachea and central airways are patent. There is a small sliding-type hiatal hernia with mild circumferential thickening of the more proximal thoracic esophagus, potentially findings of reflux esophagitis. Correlation with the patient's history and signs / symptoms of potential gastroesophageal reflux disease is suggested. Limited images of the abdomen are unremarkable. Bones and soft tissues are unremarkable. IMPRESSION: PATTERN OF LUNG DISEASE PRIMARILY CHARACTERIZED BY DIFFUSE MOSAIC PARENCHYMAL ATTENUATION WITH PROBABLE AIR-TRAPPING (EXPIRATORY PHASE IMAGING NOT PERFORMED) AND IRREGULAR LINEAR OPACITIES WITH RELATIVE PRESERVATION OF LUNG ARCHITECTURE AND LACK OF FRANK HONEYCOMBING. FINDINGS ARE ATYPICAL FOR USUAL INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA (UIP) AND MAY BE MORE CHARACTERISTIC OF CHRONIC HYPERSENSITIVITY PNEUMONITIS, DRUG-INDUCED LUNG INJURY, OR PERHAPS COLLAGEN VASCULAR DISEASE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: The thyroid gland is heterogeneous with enlargement of the left thyroid lobe. A 1.1 cm and 6 mm hypodense nodule is present on the left. Post-operative changes to the left breast and axilla are seen. Surrounding fat necrosis is likely present, but correlation with mammography is recommended. There is interval increase in size of multiple lymph nodes throughout the superior mediastinum and chest. Representative nodes include a 9-mm node located anterior to the right subclavian artery and posterior to the thyroid gland (series 2, image 309); a 16 mm left subpectoral lymph node (series 2, image 280), previously 14 mm; a 11 mm node anterior to the left clavicle (series 2, image 302), previously 6 mm; a node adjacent to the left thyroid lobe (series 2, image 329); and multiple bilateral cardiophrenic nodes. Additionally soft tissue in the anterior mediastinum appears worsened. For example, along the right internal mammary artery there is soft tissue measuring 1.7 x 2.2 cm (series 2, image 256). In the left anterior mediastinum, a soft tissue mass / node measuring 11 mm is identified (series 2, image 262). Right Mediport is present which terminates in the cavoatrial junction. The heart size is normal without a pericardial effusion. The aorta is normal in course and caliber. The pulmonary arterial tree is well opacified, without filling defects to suggest pulmonary embolism. The main pulmonary artery is normal in course and caliber. The lungs demonstrate bibasilar atelectasis. Plaque-like pleural thickening and nodularity along the left hemidiaphragm is seen with an associated small effusion. The airways are patent and of normal course and caliber. No mediastinal, hilar or axillary lymphadenopathy. Status post cholecystectomy. A lymph node adjacent to the left adrenal gland is new since July 4th, 18 and measures 9 mm (series 2, image 26). Stable appearance of small porta hepatis nodes. Left sided sclerosis of the sternum is seen, similar to 12/17/2017 though increased compared to more remote priors. Historically demonstrated presternal soft tissue lesion is not seen though there is similar thin, focal areas of soft tissue attenuation in this region compared to the prior examination. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. INTERVAL POST OPERATIVE CHANGES TO THE LEFT BREAST AND AXILLA. INTERVAL INCREASE IN SIZE OF MULTIPLE NODES IN THE ANTERIOR CHEST AND SUPERIOR MEDIASTINUM WHICH MAY BE REACTIVE DUE TO RECENT SURGERY VERSUS REPRESENT PROGRESSION OF DISEASE. 3. LEFT PLEURAL THICKENING AND NODULARITY WITH AN INCREASING PLEURAL EFFUSION. FINDINGS ARE WORRISOME FOR PLEURAL METASTASES WITH A MALIGNANT EFFUSION. 4. INCREASE IN SIZE SINCE 6/19/2018 OF A LYMPH NODE ADJACENT TO THE LEFT ADRENAL GLAND MEASURING 9 MM, NONSPECIFIC. 5. STABLE STERNAL SCLEROSIS WHICH MAY REFLECT POST-TREATMENT CHANGE VS. AN OSSEOUS METASTASES. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: CARDIOVASCULAR: Heart: Normal size. No pericardial effusion. No traumatic injury. Aorta / Branch vessels: Aorta is normal caliber without evidence of traumatic injury, aneurysm or dissection. No mediastinal hematoma. Three vessel arch. The great vessels are patent. The right subclavian artery is patent along its entire course. The internal mammary arteries are patent and intact bilaterally. OTHER CHEST: Patient is status post gunshot wound to the right chest. There is subcutaneous gas and hematoma in the right chest wall (intra-and sub-pectoral), but no evidence of active arterial extravasation. Severely comminuted fractures of the anterior third and fourth ribs. In the subjacent right upper lobe, there is a large area of pulmonary hemorrhage / contusion and multiple traumatic pneumatoceles / laceration. No definite retained bullet fragments. A small right pneumothorax. Moderate right hemothorax. Layering dependently within the hemothorax is a corticated 4-mm bone fragment, likely migrated from a shattered rib. The left lung is clear. Pulmonary arterial tree is well opacified; no active pulmonary arterial extravasation. Minimal soft tissue density in the anterior mediastinum is felt to represent thymus. No pneumomediastinum. Comminuted fracture of the right proximal humerus is incompletely imaged on this study. No other fractures are present in the chest. For findings related to abdomen pelvis, please refer to separately dictated report. Addendum Begins 3D reformations consisting of curved and multiplanar reformations, maximum intensity projections, and volume rendered images were performed on an independent workstation and corroborate the findings. Addendum Ends IMPRESSION: 1. BALLISTIC TRAUMA TO THE RIGHT ANTERIOR CHEST, RESULTING IN LARGE RIGHT UPPER LOBE CONTUSION WITH TRAUMATIC PNEUMATOCELES / LACERATION, 2. SMALL RIGHT PNEUMOTHORAX, MODERATE RIGHT HEMOTHORAX. 3. SEVERELY COMMINUTED FRACTURES OF THE ANTERIOR THIRD AND FOURTH RIBS. SMALL BONE FRAGMENT LAYERS DEPENDENTLY IN THE RIGHT HEMOTHORAX. NO DEFINITE RETAINED BULLET FRAGMENTS SEEN. 4. NO TRAUMATIC AORTIC OR SUPRA-ARCH VESSEL INJURY. NO ACTIVE ARTERIAL EXTRAVASATION IN THE VISUALIZED VESSELS. 5. COMMINUTED PROXIMAL RIGHT HUMERUS FRACTURE IS NOT COMPLETELY IMAGED ON THIS STUDY. RECOMMEND DEDICATED IMAGING. Findings were discussed with M. D. Kellan E of trauma surgery at 1325 hours on 2-22-2004 by PENA MIRIAM PAUL PA of radiology. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

Pulmonary Vasculature: There is no evidence of pulmonary embolus in the main, lobar, or segmental pulmonary arteries. input: FINDINGS: Small bilateral pleural effusions are present with dependent atelectasis. There is no evidence of pulmonary embolism within the main and segmental pulmonary arteries; however, evaluation of the subsegmental arteries is limited by respiratory motion and patient body habitus. There are multiple calcified infrahilar and azygoesophageal lymph nodes. There is mild narrowing of the right mainstem bronchus. Narrowing of the left brachiocephalic vein as well as the SVC are noted. There is reflux of contrast into the superior intercostal vein. A right internal jugular venous catheter is in place with tip in the SVC. The heart demonstrates aortic valve calcifications. No significant pericardial effusion. Visualized portions of the upper abdomen are grossly unremarkable. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM WITHIN THE MAIN AS WELL AS LOBAR AND SEGMENTAL PULMONARY ARTERIES. EVALUATION OF THE SUBSEGMENTAL ARTERIES IS LIMITED BY RESPIRATORY MOTION AND PATIENT'S BODY HABITUS. 2. SMALL BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS WITH ASSOCIATED ATELECTASIS. 3. NARROWING OF THE LEFT BRACHIOCEPHALIC VEIN AS WELL AS SVC. 4. CALCIFIED RIGHT INFRAHILAR AND AZYGOESOPHAGEAL LYMPH NODES. 5. PRELIMINARY FINDINGS WERE CALLED TO Carey Pierce AT 11:30 AM ON 6/28/2018 BY THE ON-CALL RESIDENT. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There is a high-grade stenosis of the superior vena cava at the confluence of the brachiocephalic veins. In addition, there is an 11 mm complete occlusion of the right brachiocephalic vein with punctate calcification in the region of the occlusion. Severe narrowing of the left brachiocephalic at the entry point to the superior vena cava is also identified with the narrowest dimension measuring approximately 4.6 mm. As a consequence of these findings, there is extensive venous collateralization involving the intercostals, lumbars, internal mammary on the right, hemiazygos and azygos veins. The subclavian veins are also enlarged. There are transhepatic venous collaterals with increased attenuation within the left lobe of the liver consistent with a perfusion abnormality. There is consistent with the hot hepatic lobe sign and can be seen with SVC obstruction. There is a large pericardial effusion with fluid and stranding seen in the perivascular mediastinum. There is para-aortic stranding and soft tissue density seen surrounding the ascending aorta which may be related to the patient's venous congestion or alternatively represent inflammatory tissue from the patient's underlying known diagnosis of lupus vasculitis. Although less likely in this clinical scenario, aortitis cannot be entirely excluded based solely on the imaging findings. Multiple nodes are also identified within the mediastinum including AP window, paratracheal and subcarinal regions. Multiple enlarged nodes are also identified within the left axilla. There is a moderate left-sided pleural effusion with associated atelectasis. A patchy focal area of air-space disease is seen within the superior lingular segment on the left which may represent a focal area of pneumonitis or early superimposed infection. The remainder of the lung parenchyma is clear. With the exception of the previously described perfusion abnormality in the left lobe of the liver, the liver is unremarkable. Both kidneys are atrophic consistent with the patient's history of chronic hemodialysis and renal transplant. The adrenals, spleen and pancreas are unremarkable. The gallbladder is decompressed. The bony structures are unremarkable. IMPRESSION: 1. HIGH-GRADE STENOSIS OF THE SUPERIOR VENA CAVA AT THE CONFLUENCE OF THE BRACHIOCEPHALIC VEINS. 2.11 MM COMPLETE OCCLUSION OF THE RIGHT BRACHIOCEPHALIC WITH SEVERE NARROWING OF THE LEFT BRACHIOCEPHALIC VEIN. 3. EXTENSIVE VENOUS COLLATERALIZATION, PARTICULARLY ON THE RIGHT SIDE INVOLVING THE INTERCOSTALS, LUMBARS, AND INTERNAL MAMMARY. THE HEMIAZYGOS, AZYGOS AND SUBCLAVIAN VEINS ARE ALSO ENLARGED. THERE IS TRANSHEPATIC VENOUS COLLATERALS WITH A PERFUSION ABNORMALITY IN THE LEFT LOBE OF THE LIVER, CONSISTENT WITH THE HEPATIC LOBE SIGN SEEN IN SVC OBSTRUCTION. 4. LARGE PERICARDIAL EFFUSION WITH FLUID, STRANDING AND ADENOPATHY SEEN WITHIN THE MEDIASTINUM. PARA-AORTIC SOFT TISSUE DENSITY WHICH MAY BE SECONDARY TO THE VENOUS CONGESTION, VERSUS INFLAMMATORY TISSUE. ALTHOUGH LESS LIKELY, AORTITIS CANNOT BE ENTIRELY EXCLUDED BASED SOLELY ON THE IMAGING FINDINGS. 5. PLEURAL EFFUSION AND ASSOCIATED ATELECTASIS WITH PATCHY AREA OF FOCAL AIR-SPACE DISEASE IN THE LEFT LINGULAR SEGMENT. 6. BILATERAL RENAL ATROPHY. SUMMARY: 4 POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION. I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

The left second rib fracture demonstrates displacement with overriding of fragments. ABDOMEN / PELVIS Liver within normal limits. Cholelithiasis. Prior proctocolectomy and right lower quadrant ileostomy. Bladder unremarkable. Tiny amount of free fluid in pelvis. No lymphadenopathy. No suspicious osseous lesions. FOCAL AREA OF TREE IN BUD OPACIFICATION IN THE RIGHT MIDDLE LOBE AND LINGULA, WHICH COULD RELATE TO INFECTION VERSUS INFLAMMATION. input: FINDINGS: The patient complained of chest pain during scan acquisition. The scan was terminated and the patient's chest pain was addressed by the code team. The motion leads to artifact degradation of the images. A right internal jugular dialysis catheter is demonstrated. The longer tip of the catheter is located in the right atrium. The shorter tip of the catheter is located in the superior vena cava. There is non-occlusive thrombosis associated with the dialysis catheter more prominently associated wtih the long limb, extending into the superior cavoatrial junction and along the extent of the catheter within the right atrium. There is also eccentric thrombus located within the right atrium extending into the right atrial appendage. There is a thrombosis located within the left atrial appendage. There are tiny foci of gas within the right ventricle and right ventricular outflow tract. There is a tiny area of gas located within the proximal ascending aorta. There is gas within the mid and distal right coronary artery and several of its branches. It is possible that this is a complication of the contrast medium injection. An event report has been filed. There is a circumferential area of endocardial low density on these 93 second delayed images. This is a nonspecific finding and may related to prior ischemic insult and resultant fibrosis. The pulmonary artery is enlarged, consistent with pulmonary arterial hypertension and measures 34 mm in short axis. The right atrium and right ventricle are enlarged as is the inferior vena cava. There is passive congestion within the liver with peripheral areas of hypervascularity, which are nonspecific but likely represent tiny shunts. Evaluation of the upper abdomen is otherwise significant for bilateral renal atrophy. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen is otherwise unremarkable. There is elevation of the left hemidiaphragm. Motion artifact limits detailed evaluation of the lung parenchyma. The lungs are clear of consolidation. There is linear opacity in the right lung base likely representing scar. There is no pleural or pericardial effusion. Osseous structures demonstrate degenerative changes. There is cervicothoracic kyphosis demonstrated. There is gas and contrast seen within the veins in the right axilla. There is a right axillary vascular stent which is incompletely visualized and demonstrates eccentric thrombus. Limited evaluation demonstrates no definite evidence of pulmonary embolism. There is no proximal coronary artery thrombosis. Evaluation for pulmonary arterial compression of the left anterior descending artery is not performed with certainty given the motion related artifact. IMPRESSION: 1. STUDY SLIGHTLY LIMITED BY MOTION ARTIFACT, DUE TO ACUTE CHEST PAIN EXPERIENCED BY THE PATIENT DURING THE CT STUDY. 2. AGAIN DEMONSTRATIED IS A CATHETER ASSOCIATED THROMBOSIS AS ABOVE DESCRIBED, WHICH IS DECREASED WHEN COMPARED TO THE PRIOR STUDY. THERE IS RESIDUAL THROMBOSIS ASSOCIATED WITH THE CATHETER AND WITHIN THE RIGHT AND LEFT ATRIA. 3. AIR EMBOLISM WITHIN THE AORTA AND RIGHT CORONARY ARTERY, AS DESCRIBED. 4. SUBENDOCARDIAL LOW ATTENUATION ON VENOUS PHASE IMAGING OF UNCLEAR CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE. 5. THESE RESULTS WERE DISCUSSED WITH tobias pa-c IMMEDIATELY FOLLOWING THE STUDY. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION

input: FINDINGS: Exam is limited due to respiratory motion at the lung bases. The visualized thyroid gland is unremarkable. The main pulmonary artery is of normal caliber and there are no filling defects within the pulmonary arteries. The heart is normal in size, with a normal branching pattern of the great vessels. No pericardial effusion. Visualized great vessels and the thoracic and visualized abdominal aorta are of normal caliber. There are prominent axillary lymph nodes bilaterally, similar to prior exam, measuring up to 6 mm in short axis diameter. A 6 millimeter right paratracheal lymph node is unchanged. No abnormally enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules are not significantly changed in size, with index nodules as follows: Left upper lobe nodule (2/393) measures 1.3 x 1.1 cm, previously 1.3 x 1.0 cm; left lower lobe nodule (2/278) currently measures 1.2 x 1.1 cm, previously 1.1 cm; right middle lobe nodule (2/350) currently measures 1.5 x 1.3 cm, previously 1.5 x 1.1 cm; right lower lobe nodule (4/27) currently measures 1.2 cm, previously 1.2 x 1.3 cm. Interval resolution of previously noted left pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. Limited evaluation of the upper abdomen in pulmonary arterial contrast phase demonstrates no gross abnormalities. Visualized osseous structures are unremarkable, without focal suspicious lesion. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM WITHIN LIMITATIONS OF THE EXAM. 2. MULTIPLE BILATERAL PULMONARY NODULES, CONSISTENT WITH METASTATIC DISEASE, ARE NOT SIGNIFICANTLY CHANGED. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are present within the supraclavicular or axillary lymph nodes stations. Multiple mediastinal lymph nodes are again seen in the paratracheal and AP window lymph node stations measuring up to 7 mm in short axis diameter, stable compared to the prior examination. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber. The main pulmonary artery is at the upper limits of normal, measuring 28 mm in diameter. The heart is normal in size. There is physiologic pericardial fluid. Calcified left hilar lymph nodes are present. The esophagus appears dilated, particularly in its distal aspect. Limited noncontrast, noncontiguous evaluation of the upper abdomen demonstrates distended stomach. There is a tiny hypodensity within the liver, which is too small to characterize. Bones and soft tissue are unremarkable. Evaluation of the lung parenchyma again demonstrates ground glass and reticulation with a peripheral and basilar predominance. In the lower lobes, ground glass and reticulation are also seen in a peribronchovascular distribution. There is associated architectural distortion and traction bronchiolectasis without significant honeycombing. Compared to prior examination, there has been no significant interval change. The central airways remain patent. No pleural effusions. IMPRESSION: 1. GROUND GLASS AND RETICULATION WITH A PERIPHERAL AND BASILAR PREDOMINANCE ASSOCIATED WITH TRACTION BRONCHIOLECTASIS AND NO SIGNIFICANT HONEYCOMBING. FINDINGS ARE AGAIN COMPATIBLE WITH NSIP RELATED TO PATIENT'S MIXED CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE. NO SIGNIFICANT CHANGE COMPARED TO PRIOR EXAMINATION. 2. REDEMONSTRATION OF DILATED ESOPHAGUS, PARTICULARLY DISTALLY. SUMMARY: 2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED

input: FINDINGS: The pulmonary artery and its branches enhance normally with contrast. There is no evidence for a pulmonary embolus. The main pulmonary artery, left and right pulmonary arteries are markedly enlarged. There is reflux of contrast into the IVC and hepatic veins. There is partial anomalous pulmonary venous return involving the lingula and left upper lobe with evidence of left to right shunting. The left and right atria and right ventricle are markedly enlarged. There is a large ASD with CT evidence of left to right shunting. There is a mild pericardial effusion. There is calcification of the aortic valve, mitral annulus, mitral valve and coronary arteries. There is a left-sided aortic arch with 3-vessel cervical branching. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber throughout its course. There is moderate atherosclerotic disease of the aortic arch and descending thoracic aorta. There are moderate bilateral pleural effusions, greater on the right than the left side with associated atelectasis. The trachea and mainstem bronchi are patent. There is a 9 mm x 1.4 cm hypoattenuating hepatic lesion in the posterior right hepatic lobe not characterized on this exam. There are multilevel degenerative changes of the spine with osteopenia, thoracic kyphosis, multilevel disk height loss, and L1 compression deformity with post vertebroplasty changes in L1 and L2. IMPRESSION: 1. NO PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. CT EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY HYPERTENSION. 3. CARDIOMEGALY WITH LARGE ASD AND PARTIAL ANOMALOUS PULMONARY VENOUS RETURN FROM THE LEFT UPPER LOBE, BOTH CAUSING LEFT TO RIGHT SHUNTING. MILD PERICARDIAL EFFUSION. 4. MODERATE BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS AND ATELECTASIS, RIGHT GREATER THAN LEFT SIDE. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

output.anchor: left-sided aortic arch with 3-vessel cervical branching (observation:present)

related: greater on the right than the left side (degree:present)

input: FINDINGS: CHEST: Note: Exam is adequate for evaluation of central pulmonary emboli, but severely limited for segmental and subsegmental branches. There is no evidence of a pulmonary embolism. Visualized portions of the thyroid are normal. Great vessel configuration is normal. The aortic arch is normal in appearance, with minimal ductus calcification. Heart size is at the upper limits of normal, with a mildly enlarged left atrium (4 cm in AP diameter). There is no pericardial effusion. There are no enlarged mediastinal, hilar, or axillary lymph nodes. Largest mediastinal lymph nodes measure 5 mm in the right paratracheal space. There is no pleural effusion or pneumothorax. With the exception of very minimal trace atelectasis, the lungs are essentially clear. There is no focal consolidation to suggest pneumonia. With the exception of a calcified granuloma in the right upper lobe measuring 4 mm, no other nodule or mass is identified. The central airways are patent. Visualized portions of the upper abdomen demonstrate diffuse hepatic steatosis. No other abnormalities are identified in the upper abdomen. BONES: There are no acute fractures. There are no suspicious lytic or sclerotic bone lesions. Thoracic spine alignment is anatomic, with mild anterior osteophyte formation. IMPRESSION: 1. TECHNICALLY LIMITED STUDY DUE TO BOLUS TIMING, BUT NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM. 2. LUNGS ARE ESSENTIALLY CLEAR, WITHOUT CT EXPLANATION FOR SHORTNESS OF BREATH. 3. DIFFUSE HEPATIC STEATOSIS. 4. THE FINAL REPORT IS CONCORDANT WITH THE PRELIMINARY REPORT PROVIDED BY THE OVERNIGHT RESIDENT IN PACS. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

input: FINDINGS: There are no filling defects within the pulmonary arterial system to suggest embolism. There are several small aortopulmonary arteries within the left hilum which may arise from the proximal descending aorta. However, no hypertrophied left or right bronchial artery is seen. Heart is within normal limits in size. There is no pericardial effusion. There is bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, similar to prior study. There is redemonstration of severe bilateral upper lobe predominant bronchiectasis, with several areas of mucoid impaction, particularly evident within the right middle lobe and right lower lobe. There are numerous tree in bud nodules within both lungs, particularly within the left lower lobe. There are new large nodular areas of consolidation within the superior segment of the right upper lobe, some associated with peripheral groundglass opacity, most compatible with pneumonia. There is no pleural effusion or pneumothorax. Limited images of the upper abdomen are grossly unremarkable. Visualized bones appear normal, aside from mild scoliosis IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY EMBOLISM. SMALL AORTOPULMONARY ARTERIES ARE PRESENT WITHIN THE LEFT HILUM, HOWEVER NO HYPERTROPHIED BRONCHIAL ARTERIES ARE VISUALIZED. 2. NEW LARGE AREAS OF NODULAR CONSOLIDATION WITHIN THE SUPERIOR SEGMENT OF THE RIGHT UPPER LOBE, MOST COMPATIBLE WITH PNEUMONIA. 3. REDEMONSTRATED FINDINGS OF CYSTIC FIBROSIS, WITH BRONCHIECTASIS, INSPISSATED AIRWAYS, AND NUMEROUS TREE-IN-BUD NODULES. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above.

related: superior segment of right upper lobe (location:present)

THE CARDIOMEDIASTINAL SILHOUETTE APPEARS NORMAL. 4. A RIGHT-SIDED CENTRAL LINE CROSSES THE MIDLINE, WITH THE TIP OVERLYING THE LEFT INNOMINATE VEIN. A RIGHT-SIDED PLEURAL DRAIN OVERLIES THE RIGHT LUNG APEX. THE ENDOTRACHEAL TUBE TIP IS 3.5 CM ABOVE THE LEVEL OF THE CARINA. THE NASOGASTRIC TUBE TIP OVERLIES THE STOMACH. SUMMARY: 1-NO SIGNIFICANT ABNORMALITY I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed with the report transcribed above. ACCESSION NUMBER: xqsb This report has been anonymized. All dates are offset from the actual dates by a fixed interval associated with the patient.

instruction:
Your task is extracting medical entities from a single radiology report and returning them as a strictly defined relation graph in JSON format.

The output must be a SINGLE JSON ARRAY of relation objects following this structure:

```json
[
  {
    "anchor": "str (field:status)",
    "related": ["str (field:status)"]
  }
]
```

Where:

- "field" is one of: "observation", "location", "degree", "trend", "measurement"
- "status" is: 
  - "present", "absent", or "uncertain" for "observation", "location", "degree", "trend" fields.
  - "current" or "old" for "measurement" field.

Do NOT output anything other than JSON array.

GENERAL RULES

Output must be a valid SINGLE JSON array.
Use only the report text provided.
If the report has Findings and Impression (or Summary, Conclusion) sections, use ONLY FINDINGS section.
If the report lacks Findings, use the given text.
Ignore History, Technique, Dose, Imaging Devices, Administrative Text, and Boilerplate Parts of the report.
Do NOT infer unstated observations, locations, degrees, trends, or measurements; extract only clinical entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
No grammatical, lexical transformation is permitted. DO NOT ALTER WORD FORMS in any way.

ENTITY FIELDS

OBSERVATION

An observation is any clinically relevant Finding, Radiological Signs & Signal Alterations, Pathological Conditions & Diseases, Morphological & Structural Abnormalities, Anatomical Status, or Surgical Hardware/Procedure documented by a radiologist.

Format in graph: "value (observation:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain".

COMPOUND OBSERVATIONS
Fixed clinical phrases must be extracted as a single observation entity. Do NOT split them into separate components.

OBSERVATION STATUS

- present: Use when the report clearly confirms the existence of an observation.
- absent: Use when a specific observation is explicitly ruled out or negated.
- uncertain: Use when the report contains equivocal, suggestive, or non-definitive language regarding the observation

LOCATION

A "location" represents the explicit Anatomical Site, Organs and Solid Structures, Hollow Organs / Luminal Structures, Anatomical Regions, Subregions / Segments / Lobes, Vascular Structures/ Lymphatic System, Anatomical Structure, Anatomical Level, or Spatial / Directional descriptor.

Format in graph: "value (location:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain"

LOCATION STATUS

present: Use when an anatomical site is explicitly described as involved or associated with an entity.

absent: Use when a specific anatomical structure is explicitly stated to be surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen etc. Do NOT extract surgically removed, not visualized, not identified, not seen phrases as observations. 

uncertain: Use when an anatomical site is not clearly visualized, not well delineated, poorly seen, obscured, partially visualized, indeterminate on imaging etc.

DEGREES

Degree stores qualifiers that characterize the Severity, Extent, Quantity, Distribution, Pattern, Appearance, Morphologic Character, Grade, Relative Laterality Comparison of an observation.

Format in graph: "value (degree:status)" where status is "present", "absent", or "uncertain`.

DEGREE STATUS

present: When the degree term is explicitly and affirmatively stated, and is NOT inside negation or uncertainty scope.

absent: When the degree qualifier itself is explicitly negated.

uncertain: When the degree qualifier is explicitly within the scope of equivocal or hedging language.

TRENDS

Trend stores Stability, Progression, Temporal Status, Temporal Context, Clinical Course / Duration, Recurrent / Re-demonstration descriptors that characterize how an observation or location entity has changed over time.

TREND STATUS

present: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly stated as present.
absent: Use when a trend descriptor is explicitly negated.
uncertain: Use when a trend descriptor is equivocal or uncertain.

MEASUREMENTS

Measurement stores numeric values like Size, Volume, Velocity, Multi-Dimensional Size, Percentages with units that quantify an observation, location.

Format in graph: "value (measurement:status)" where status is "current" or "old`.

MEASUREMENT STATUS

current: Use when the measurement refers to the present study.
old: Use when the measurement refers to a prior study or baseline value.

ABBREVIATIONS

Abbreviations (e.g., sbo, cad, mac, ivh, sdh, edh, sah, cac, ivc) MUST be extracted exactly as written. If an abbreviation represents a pathological finding, clinical sign, or condition, extract it as an Observation. If an abbreviation represents an anatomical structure or region, extract it as a Location. Apply the standard presence/absence/uncertainty logic to the entity type.

NEGATED DESCRIPTORS

Phrases like "not enlarged", "non-calcified" must be extracted without the negation prefix, assigned status = "absent" under the appropriate field.

ANCHOR TYPE RESTRICTION RULE

An anchor node MUST be either: an observation OR a location (only when no observation exists in that sentence context). A trend, degree, or measurement can NEVER be used as an anchor under any circumstance. Trend, degree, or measurement values MUST NEVER be converted into observations.

FINAL INSTRUCTION

Extract medical entities from the given report and return ONLY the relation graph as a JSON array.
input: FINDINGS: A decreased degree of inspiration compared to the prior exam. A skin fold seen superiorly on the left is noted but it does not represent pneumothorax. In the left lung, a small 8-mm nodule is noted which was also present on the prior exam of 5-24-2001. In the right lower lobe in the region overlying anterior ribs 5 and 6, there are two areas of near density, the inferior of these being about 2 cm in diameter with rather straight margins. The regions were not present on the prior examination. IMPRESSION: 1. SMALL LEFT LUNG NODULAR DENSITY SEEN LATERALLY, OLD, SEEN ON 5-24-2001 EXAM. 2. TWO AREAS OF RIGHT LOWER LOBE DENSITY, AS DESCRIBED, NOT IDENTIFIED ON THE PRIOR EXAM OF 05/24. SUPERIOR OF THESE SUGGESTIVE OF ATELECTASIS, BUT THE INFERIOR 2-CM DENSITY APPEARANCE IS COMPLETELY NONSPECIFIC. VERY DIFFICULT TO RULE OUT PNEUMONIA IN THIS REGION, AND CLINICAL CORRELATION AND A CHEST PA AND LATERAL SUGGESTED FOR BETTER EVALUATION. SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION ACCESSION NUMBER: 9926321246 This report has been anonymized. All dates are offset from the actual dates by a fixed interval associated with the patient.

input: FINDINGS: The cardiomediastinal silhouette is stable with tortuosity redemonstrated in the thoracic aorta. The lungs are clear without evidence of focal consolidation. IMPRESSION: 1. NO EVIDENCE OF ACUTE CARDIOPULMONARY DISEASE. 2. REDEMONSTRATION OF LYTIC LESIONS WITHIN THE CLAVICLES BILATERALLY AND A HEALING DISTAL LEFT CLAVICULAR FRACTURE. THERE IS DEGENERATIVE OR POSTTRAUMATIC DEFORMITY OF THE RIGHT HUMERAL HEAD. DEGENERATIVE CHANGES ARE NOTED INVOLVING THE SPINE AND ACROMIOCLAVICULAR JOINTS. THERE IS STABLE LIKELY POST TRAUMATIC DEFORMITY OF THE SEVENTH LEFT POSTERIOR LATERAL RIB. THE OSSEOUS STRUCTURES ARE MILDLY OSTEOPENIC.
